**The Six Principles (4 marks — 0.5 mark each, plus 1 mark for synthesis):**

1. **Practical (not necessarily mathematical) indecipherability.**
   A cipher need not be information-theoretically unbreakable, but it must be *computationally* infeasible for an adversary with bounded resources.

2. **No secrecy of the system required.**
   The cipher design may be captured by the enemy without compromising security. This is the *Kerckhoffs Doctrine* and is the cornerstone of modern open-source cryptography.

3. **Key communicability and changeability.**
   Keys must be transmittable, storable, and revocable without reliance on long written notes. This drives modern **PKI (Public Key Infrastructure)** and **KMS (Key Management Systems)**.

4. **Applicability to telegraphic correspondence.**
   The cipher must operate over communication channels (originally telegraph, today the Internet) — it must support **digital transmission** with no physical dependency.

5. **Portability and single-user operation.**
   The system must function without the coordinated effort of multiple agents. Modern: a smartphone with hardware crypto module (TPM, Secure Enclave) satisfies this.

6. **Ease of use.**
   The system must require neither mental strain nor memorization of long rules. Modern HCI analogy: PGP-style end-to-end encryption is too complex for most users — this principle motivates the design of user-friendly protocols like Signal.

**Conclusion / Relevance to Modern Design (2 marks):**
Each of the six principles maps directly to a **modern cryptographic requirement**: computational security (P1), open standardization (P2), key management infrastructure (P3), protocol-layer compatibility (P4), end-user device integration (P5), and usability engineering (P6). The NIST Cryptographic Standards and Guidelines, ISO/IEC 19790, and FIPS 140-3 all implicitly derive from these 19th-century axioms.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — Modern Implications with AES & RSA (7 marks)

**Open Design (2 marks):**
Kerckhoffs's Principle demands that algorithms be public. AES (FIPS 197, 2001) and RSA (RFC 8017) are fully published, peer-reviewed, and standardized. AES was selected via an **open NIST competition (1997–2000)** with public cryptanalysis — a textbook application of the Doctrine.

**Key Secrecy as the Sole Defense (2 marks):**
- For **AES-128**, security reduces to the secrecy of the 128-bit key. The adversary is given the algorithm and tries all $2^{128}$ keys — computationally infeasible. Compromise is recoverable via **key rotation**.
- For **RSA-2048**, security reduces to the difficulty of factoring a 2048-bit semiprime. Even with the algorithm public, breaking it requires solving a hard number-theoretic problem.

**Peer Review = Strength (1.5 marks):**
Since AES and RSA are public, they have been subjected to **decades of cryptanalysis** by the global community (IACR conferences: CRYPTO, EUROCRYPT, ASIACRYPT). This continuous scrutiny has *increased* confidence, not decreased it. A5/1 (GSM) — kept secret — was reverse-engineered in 1999 and found to be weak.

**Algorithm Agility & Standardization (1.5 marks):**
Because the algorithm is not the secret, the community can transition from DES → 3DES → AES → AES-GCM without disruption to the security model. The same is true for RSA-1024 → RSA-2048 → RSA-3072. This is **algorithm agility**, an operational benefit impossible under security-through-obscurity.

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Define Kerckhoffs's Principle formally. Express it in terms of the 5-tuple cryptosystem $(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{C}, \mathcal{K}, \mathcal{E}, \mathcal{D})$ and the IND-CPA security game. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Shannon's Maxim** and its mathematical formulation. Why is **information-theoretic security** (e.g., the One-Time Pad) considered the strongest possible realization of Kerckhoffs's Principle? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — Formal Definition & IND-CPA (7 marks)

**Formal Definition (2 marks):**
A cryptosystem $\mathcal{S} = (\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{C}, \mathcal{K}, \mathcal{E}, \mathcal{D})$ satisfies Kerckhoffs's Principle if, for any probabilistic polynomial-time (PPT) adversary $\mathcal{A}$ that has full knowledge of $\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{C}, \mathcal{E}, \mathcal{D}$, the probability of recovering the plaintext $p$ from ciphertext $c = E_k(p)$ without knowing $k$ is at most $\tfrac{1}{2} + \epsilon(\lambda)$, where $\epsilon(\lambda)$ is negligible.

**5-Tuple Elaboration (2 marks):**
- $\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{C}$ — public; known to all.
- $\mathcal{E}, \mathcal{D}$ — public algorithms; documented in standards (FIPS, RFC).
- $\mathcal{K}$ — partially known (structure), but the specific $k$ is secret.
- The tuple itself may be published openly (e.g., in a research paper) without weakening the system.

**IND-CPA Game Formalization (3 marks):**
The Indistinguishability under Chosen-Plaintext Attack (IND-CPA) game is the standard formalization of Kerckhoffs's Principle:

| Step | Action | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Challenger picks $k \leftarrow^{\$} \mathcal{K}$ | Random key generation |
| 2 | Adversary $\mathcal{A}$ queries encryption oracle $E_k(\cdot)$ | Algorithm is fully accessible |
| 3 | $\mathcal{A}$ outputs $(m_0, m_1)$ of equal length | Chosen plaintext pair |
| 4 | Challenger picks $b \leftarrow^{\$} \{0, 1\}$ | Random bit |
| 5 | Challenger returns $c^* = E_k(m_b)$ | Challenge ciphertext |
| 6 | $\mathcal{A}$ outputs guess $b'$ | Adversary decides |

The advantage is:
$$
\text{Adv}^{\text{ind-cpa}}_{\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}}(\lambda) = \left| \Pr[b' = b] - \tfrac{1}{2} \right|
$$

Kerckhoffs's Principle demands $\text{Adv}^{\text{ind-cpa}} \leq \epsilon(\lambda)$ — a **negligible function** in security parameter $\lambda$.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — Shannon's Maxim & OTP (7 marks)

**Shannon's Maxim (2.5 marks):**
Claude Shannon, in *"Communication Theory of Secrecy Systems"* (1949, Bell System Technical Journal), restated Kerckhoffs's doctrine as:

> *"One ought to design systems under the assumption that the enemy will immediately gain full familiarity with them."*

This is the **adversarial model** underlying all modern provable security definitions (IND-CPA, IND-CCA, EUF-CMA, etc.). Mathematically, it is the **worst-case assumption**: security must hold even when $\mathcal{A}$ is given *everything* except the key.

**Information-Theoretic Security (2.5 marks):**
A scheme is **information-theoretically secure (ITS)** if, for any adversary (even with unbounded computation):

$$
\Pr[p \mid c] = \Pr[p], \quad \forall\, p \in \mathcal{P}, \forall\, c \in \mathcal{C}.
$$

That is, observing $c$ gives the adversary **zero** additional information about $p$. The mutual information $I(p; c) = 0$.

**One-Time Pad as the Strongest Realization (2 marks):**
The **Vernam cipher (One-Time Pad, OTP)** satisfies:
- Key $k$ is **truly random**, $\vert k \vert = \vert p \vert$, and used **exactly once**.
- Encryption: $c = p \oplus k$ (bitwise XOR).
- Decryption: $p = c \oplus k$.
- Given $c$, every plaintext $p'$ of the same length is *equally likely* — no adversary advantage, no matter the computational power.

The OTP is therefore the **strongest possible** realization of Kerckhoffs's Principle: the algorithm is trivial (a single XOR), but the secret key — being truly random, as long as the message, and unrepeatable — provides *perfect* security under Shannon's model. Its only weakness is **key distribution**, which is the operational trade-off that public-key cryptography (Diffie-Hellman, 1976) was created to solve.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Spelling:** It is *Kerckhoffs* (with two f's, two h's), **NOT** *Kirchhoff* (which is a German physicist's name associated with circuit laws). Examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for incorrect spelling in descriptive answers.
> 2. **Confusing Kerckhoffs's Principle with Shannon's Theorem:** Students often blur the doctrine with Shannon's *channel-capacity* theorem. The Doctrine is a *design philosophy*; Shannon's theorem is a *coding result*. State them separately.
> 3. **Forgetting the 5-tuple definition:** A formal answer to "Define Kerckhoffs's Principle" *must* mention $\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{C}, \mathcal{K}, \mathcal{E}, \mathcal{D}$ to score full marks.
> 4. **"Caesar cipher" trap:** Never cite the Caesar cipher as an example *of* Kerckhoffs's Principle. It **violates** the principle. Cite AES/RSA instead.
> 5. **In Q.B(a), stating IND-CPA without the formal `Adv` expression** loses 2 marks. The equation $\vert \Pr[b' = b] - \tfrac{1}{2} \vert$ is mandatory.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1 / Kerckhoffs's Principle**

- ✅ The principle is named after **Auguste Kerckhoffs** (1883, *"La Cryptographie Militaire"*) — **not** Kirchhoff.
- ✅ One-line definition: *"Security depends only on the key, not the algorithm."*
- ✅ Cryptosystem is a **5-tuple** $(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{C}, \mathcal{K}, \mathcal{E}, \mathcal{D})$.
- ✅ The Doctrine is the **second of six** original principles (P2). Remember all six.
- ✅ **Shannon's Maxim (1949)** is a modern restatement: assume the adversary has full knowledge.
- ✅ Modern formalization is the **IND-CPA security game**: $\text{Adv} \leq \epsilon(\lambda)$.
- ✅ $\epsilon(\lambda)$ is **negligible** in the security parameter $\lambda$.
- ✅ Key length determines brute-force effort: $\mathcal{O}(2^{\vert k \vert})$.
- ✅ **AES-128, RSA-2048, ChaCha20** = Kerckhoffs-compliant (algorithm public).
- ✅ **Caesar cipher, A5/1 (GSM), proprietary algorithms** = security through obscurity (rejected).
- ✅ The **One-Time Pad** is the strongest Kerckhoffs-compliant scheme (information-theoretically secure, $I(p;c) = 0$).
- ✅ **Public design → peer review → stronger algorithms** is the central engineering argument.
- ✅ **Algorithm agility** (DES → AES) is possible *only* under Kerckhoffs-compliant design.
- ✅ Key management (PKI, KMS) is the operational consequence of the Doctrine.
- ✅ Spelling + 5-tuple + 6 principles + Shannon reference = full-marks recipe in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Symmetric Cipher Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET746 - CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Symmetric Cipher Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Symmetric Cipher Model

A **Symmetric Cipher Model** is a cryptographic framework in which the **same secret key** is used for both the **encryption** (converting plaintext to ciphertext) and **decryption** (converting ciphertext back to plaintext) processes. Formally, it is a tuple of five elements:

$$ \mathcal{S} = (P, C, K, E, D) $$

Where:
- $P$ = Finite set of all possible **Plaintexts**
- $C$ = Finite set of all possible **Ciphertexts**
- $K$ = Finite set of all possible **Keys** (the **Keyspace**)
- $E$ = **Encryption function** such that $E_k : P \rightarrow C$
- $D$ = **Decryption function** such that $D_k : C \rightarrow P$

The defining constraint is:
$$ D_k(E_k(P)) = P \quad \text{for all} \quad P \in \mathcal{P}, k \in \mathcal{K} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The symmetric model forms the *foundational primitive* of all classical and modern block/stream ciphers (DES, AES, RC4). Every later module in PEAET746 builds upon this 5-tuple model.

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Information Security Terminology

| Term | Scope | Intuitive Meaning |
|------|-------|-------------------|
| **Cryptography** | Greek: *kryptos* (hidden) + *graphein* (write) | The art/science of designing secure ciphers |
| **Cryptanalysis** | Greek: *kryptos* + *analys* (loosen) | The art of breaking ciphers without the key |
| **Cryptology** | The umbrella discipline | Cryptography + Cryptanalysis combined |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Locked Box" Mental Model

Imagine you and your friend want to exchange secret love letters at school. You buy **two identical keys** for a single padlock. You lock the letter in a box using your copy of the key, send the box to your friend, and they open it with their **identical copy**. This is precisely the symmetric model — the *same* secret key secures *both* directions of communication. The challenge? **How do you safely share that single key in the first place?** (This is called the *key distribution problem*, and it is the central weakness of symmetric ciphers — more on this in Module 2.)

## 1.4 The Five Functional Components of the Symmetric Model

> [!NOTE]
> Every symmetric cipher, whether the ancient **Caesar cipher** or modern **AES-256**, must implement these five components. Examiners frequently pose a 3-mark question asking students to "draw and explain the symmetric cipher model."

1. **Plaintext ($P$)** — The original, readable message (e.g., `"ATTACK AT DAWN"`).
2. **Encryption Algorithm ($E_k$)** — Performs mathematical substitutions and permutations on $P$ using key $k$.
3. **Secret Key ($k$)** — A string of bits, independent of the plaintext, that controls the transformation. *Crucially*, the security must reside entirely in the key, **never** in the algorithm's secrecy.
4. **Ciphertext ($C$)** — The scrambled, unreadable output: $C = E_k(P)$.
5. **Decryption Algorithm ($D_k$)** — The inverse of $E_k$: $P = D_k(C)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kerckhoff's Principle (1883):** *"The security of a cryptosystem must not depend on keeping the algorithm secret; it must depend only on keeping the key secret."* This is the bedrock assumption of modern cryptography and is **frequently tested** in KTU ESE.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Generalized Symmetric Encryption Schematic

The end-to-end model involves **two parties** (a sender "Alice" and a receiver "Bob") sharing a *secure channel* for key exchange, while using an *insecure channel* (e.g., the internet) for the encrypted message itself.

### Operational Flow (Bulleted Logic)

1. **Alice** generates plaintext message $P$ in human-readable form.
2. She inputs $P$ and a secret key $k$ into the encryption algorithm $E_k$.
3. The algorithm outputs ciphertext $C = E_k(P)$. This is transmitted over an **insecure channel** (public).
4. An eavesdropper (Eve) may intercept $C$, but without $k$ she cannot recover $P$ in polynomial time.
5. **Bob** receives $C$ and applies decryption $D_k(C)$ using his identical copy of $k$, recovering $P$.
6. A **cryptanalyst** (Eve) attempts to recover $P$ or $k$ through three classical attack vectors:
   - **Ciphertext-Only Attack (COA):** Eve has only $C$.
   - **Known-Plaintext Attack (KPA):** Eve has pairs of $(P, C)$.
   - **Chosen-Plaintext Attack (CPA):** Eve can submit chosen $P$ to obtain $C$.

## 2.2 Requirements for Secure Symmetric Encryption

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite (3-mark question):** *"List the requirements for secure symmetric encryption."* Memorize the four pillars below.

1. **Strong Encryption Algorithm** — The algorithm should be computationally infeasible to invert without $k$.
2. **Shared Secret Key** — Sender and receiver must possess $k$ *before* secure communication begins.
3. **Confidential Key Distribution** — A secure channel (out-of-band, physically secure, or protected by asymmetric crypto) must be used to deliver $k$.
4. **Efficient Implementation** — The algorithm must run fast enough for the target application (software or hardware).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Unit / Domain |
|------------------|---------|---------------|
| $C = E_k(P)$ | Encryption transformation | $C \in \mathcal{C}$ |
| $P = D_k(E_k(P))$ | Decryption identity | $P \in \mathcal{P}$ |
| $\vert \mathcal{K} \vert$ | Size of keyspace (number of possible keys) | Dimensionless integer |
| $T_{\text{brute}} = \vert \mathcal{K} \vert \times T_{\text{test}}$ | Brute-force search time | Seconds (depends on $T_{\text{test}}$) |
| $\log_2 \vert \mathcal{K} \vert$ | Key length in bits | **bits** |
| Shannon's condition: $H(K) \geq H(P)$ | Unbreakable (one-time pad) | Information-theoretic |
| Confusion | Each key bit affects many ciphertext bits | Shannon's principle #1 |
| Diffusion | Each plaintext bit affects many ciphertext bits | Shannon's principle #2 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $\log_2 \vert \mathcal{K} \vert$ matters:** This is the **effective key length in bits**. AES-128 has a keyspace of $2^{128}$, which at 1 billion keys/sec would take $\approx 10^{22}$ years to brute-force. KTU exam questions often ask: *"Why is key length critical?"* — Answer: it makes exhaustive key search computationally infeasible.

## 2.4 The Two Classical Construction Techniques

Symmetric ciphers are built from two fundamental operations, often **combined** in modern designs:

- **Substitution (Confusion):** Each plaintext element (bit, letter, block) is mapped to a *unique* ciphertext element. Example: Caesar shift, monoalphabetic substitution tables.
- **Transposition (Diffusion):** Plaintext elements are *rearranged* (permuted), keeping the symbol set intact. Example: Rail Fence cipher, columnar transposition.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Trap:** A cipher using *only* substitution is vulnerable to frequency analysis. A cipher using *only* transposition preserves letter frequencies. **Modern ciphers (AES, DES) use iterated rounds that alternate substitution and permutation (S-P networks)** for maximum strength.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

| Domain | Symmetric Cipher Used | Why Symmetric? |
|--------|----------------------|----------------|
| HTTPS/TLS | AES-128/256 (in bulk data channel) | **Speed** — orders of magnitude faster than RSA |
| Wi-Fi (WPA3) | AES-CCMP | Hardware acceleration on every chip |
| Banking (PIN blocks) | Triple DES (3DES) → AES | Long-term data at rest, needs decades of security |
| File Encryption (BitLocker) | AES-XTS | Performance on large storage volumes |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Symmetric Cipher Model — Mathematical Derivation

We start from first principles and derive the *mathematical identity* that every symmetric cipher must satisfy.

### Step 1: Define the Plaintext, Ciphertext, and Key Spaces

$$ \mathcal{P} = \{ p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n \}, \quad \mathcal{C} = \{ c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_m \}, \quad \mathcal{K} = \{ k_1, k_2, \ldots, k_r \} $$

These are finite alphabets. For Caesar cipher: $\mathcal{P} = \mathcal{C} = \mathcal{K} = \mathbb{Z}_{26}$.

### Step 2: Define the Encryption Map

For each $k \in \mathcal{K}$, the encryption map is a function $E_k : \mathcal{P} \rightarrow \mathcal{C}$. Because we must be able to *recover* the plaintext, $E_k$ must be a **bijection** (one-to-one and onto). A non-bijective map would cause **ambiguity** during decryption.

### Step 3: Define the Decryption Map

The decryption function $D_k : \mathcal{C} \rightarrow \mathcal{P}$ is defined as the **functional inverse** of $E_k$:

$$ D_k = E_k^{-1} \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad D_k(E_k(p)) = p \quad \forall p \in \mathcal{P} $$

### Step 4: State the Decryption Identity (Consistency Condition)

$$ \boxed{ D_k(E_k(p)) = p \quad \text{for all} \quad p \in \mathcal{P}, \, k \in \mathcal{K} } $$

This is the **defining algebraic identity** of any valid symmetric cipher. If a proposed scheme violates it, the cipher is broken by design.

### Step 5: Derive the Brute-Force Search Bound

Given ciphertext $C$, an attacker Eve may try all $\vert \mathcal{K} \vert$ possible keys. The expected number of trials to find the correct key is:

$$ E[T_{\text{brute}}] = \frac{\vert \mathcal{K} \vert}{2} $$

This is the **average-case complexity**. For an $n$-bit key, $\vert \mathcal{K} \vert = 2^n$, so:

$$ E[T_{\text{brute}}] = 2^{n-1} \text{ trials} $$

### Step 6: Time Complexity in Real-World Units

Let $T_{\text{test}}$ = time to test one key (in seconds). Then the total brute-force time is:

$$ T_{\text{total}} = 2^{n-1} \times T_{\text{test}} $$

**Example derivation for DES ($n = 56$ bits):**

$$ T_{\text{total}} = 2^{55} \times 1\mu s = \frac{3.6 \times 10^{16} \mu s}{1\mu s} = 3.6 \times 10^{10} \text{ seconds} \approx 1140 \text{ years} $$

This is the very reason DES was retired and replaced by AES in 2001.

### Step 7: Shannon's Perfect Secrecy Condition

For a cipher to be **information-theoretically unbreakable** (Shannon, 1949), the key must be:

$$ H(K) \geq H(P) $$

Where $H(\cdot)$ denotes **Shannon entropy** in bits. This forces the key to be at least as long as the plaintext — the principle behind the **One-Time Pad (OTP)**.

## 3.2 The Caesar Cipher — Worked Numerical Derivation

A Caesar cipher with shift $k$ operates on letters (encoded as integers $0$ to $25$):

**Encryption derivation:**

$$ c = E_k(p) = (p + k) \pmod{26} $$

**Decryption derivation (inversion):**

$$ p = D_k(c) = (c - k) \pmod{26} $$

**Verify the identity:**

$$ D_k(E_k(p)) = ((p + k) \bmod 26 - k) \bmod 26 $$

$$ = (p + k - k) \bmod 26 = p \bmod 26 = p \quad \checkmark $$

**Worked Example:** Encrypt $P = \text{"HELLO"}$ with $k = 3$ (Julius Caesar's original shift).

- H (7) → $7 + 3 = 10$ → K
- E (4) → $4 + 3 = 7$ → H
- L (11) → $11 + 3 = 14$ → O
- L (11) → $14$ → O
- O (14) → $14 + 3 = 17$ → R

$$ C = \text{"KHOOR"} $$

**Keyspace size:** $\vert \mathcal{K} \vert = 26$ (only 26 shifts). Brute force takes microseconds — **Caesar is broken.**

## 3.3 The Vigenère Cipher — Analytical Derivation

Vigenère uses a *repeating* keyword $K = k_1 k_2 \ldots k_m$. The $i$-th plaintext letter is shifted by $k_{(i \bmod m)}$.

**Encryption formula:**

$$ c_i = (p_i + k_{i \bmod m}) \pmod{26} $$

**Decryption formula:**

$$ p_i = (c_i - k_{i \bmod m}) \pmod{26} $$

**Keyspace size derivation:**

$$ \vert \mathcal{K} \vert = 26^m \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{key length in bits} = m \log_2 26 \approx 4.7m $$

For a 10-letter keyword: $\vert \mathcal{K} \vert = 26^{10} \approx 1.4 \times 10^{14}$, which is ~47 bits — breakable in hours on modern hardware via **Kasiski examination** (repeated bigrams reveal keyword length).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generalized Symmetric Cipher Model (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **end-to-end functional flow** of a symmetric encryption system, including attacker positions, the secure key channel, and the insecure message channel — exactly the schematic KTU expects you to reproduce in the exam.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ALICE["ALICE (Sender)"]
        P1[Plaintext P] --> E1["Encryption Algorithm E_k"]
        K1[Secret Key k] --> E1
        E1 --> C1["Ciphertext C = E_k(P)"]
    end

    C1 -. "Insecure Public Channel (e.g., Internet)" .-> RX

    subgraph BOB["BOB (Receiver)"]
        RX["Received Ciphertext C"] --> D1["Decryption Algorithm D_k"]
        K2[Secret Key k identical] --> D1
        D1 --> P2[Recovered Plaintext P]
    end

    subgraph SECURE["SECURE KEY CHANNEL (Out-of-Band)"]
        KGEN["Key Generator / KDC"] -- "key k delivered securely" --> K1
        KGEN -- "key k delivered securely" --> K2
    end

    subgraph ATTACKER["EVE (Cryptanalyst)"]
        EAVES["Intercepts C on insecure channel"]
        COA["Ciphertext-Only Attack"]
        KPA["Known-Plaintext Attack"]
        CPA["Chosen-Plaintext Attack"]
    end

    C1 -. "observed by" .-> EAVES
    EAVES --> COA
    EAVES --> KPA
    EAVES --> CPA
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Map of Classical Symmetric Ciphers

This diagram shows the **taxonomy** of substitution and transposition ciphers that fall under the symmetric model. KTU Module 1 typically expects students to classify each classical cipher correctly.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT["Symmetric Cipher Model"] --> SUB["Substitution Ciphers"]
    ROOT --> TRANS["Transposition Ciphers"]
    ROOT --> PROD["Product Ciphers (SP-Networks)"]

    SUB --> MONO["Monoalphabetic"]
    SUB --> POLY["Polyalphabetic"]

    MONO --> CAESAR["Caesar Shift"]
    MONO --> AFFINE["Affine Cipher"]
    MONO --> ATBASH["Atbash"]
    MONO --> RANDOM["Random Substitution Table"]

    POLY --> VIG["Vigenere"]
    POLY --> BEAUF["Beaufort"]
    POLY --> AUTOK["Autokey Vigenere"]
    POLY --> OTP["One-Time Pad"]

    TRANS --> RAIL["Rail Fence"]
    TRANS --> COL["Columnar Transposition"]
    TRANS --> ROUTE["Route Cipher"]

    PROD --> SDES["Simplified DES (S-DES)"]
    PROD --> DES["DES"]
    PROD --> AES["AES (Rijndael)"]
```

## 4.3 Confusion vs. Diffusion — Operational View

This diagram illustrates how **Shannon's two design principles** (confusion and diffusion) are *implemented* in a single round of a modern block cipher. Every classical substitution contributes *confusion*; every permutation step contributes *diffusion*.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PB["Plaintext Block"] --> STEP1["1. Add Round Key (XOR with subkey)"]
    STEP1 --> STEP2["2. Substitute Bytes (Confusion Layer)"]
    STEP2 --> STEP3["3. Shift Rows / Permute Bits (Diffusion Layer)"]
    STEP3 --> STEP4["4. Add Round Key Again"]
    STEP4 --> ROUNDEND["Output of Round 1"]
    ROUNDEND -. "feeds into Round 2" .-> STEP1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain the symmetric cipher model with a diagram" in a 7-mark or 14-mark KTU question, reproduce the architecture in **Section 4.1** above. Label the **secure channel** for the key and the **insecure channel** for ciphertext — this is what full-marks answers do.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define a symmetric cipher model. List its five essential components.**

**Model Answer:**

A symmetric cipher model is a cryptographic system in which the **same secret key** is used for both encryption and decryption. It can be formally represented as a 5-tuple $(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{C}, \mathcal{K}, E, D)$, satisfying the identity $D_k(E_k(p)) = p$ for all $p \in \mathcal{P}$.

The **five essential components** are:
1. **Plaintext ($\mathcal{P}$)** — Original readable message.
2. **Encryption Algorithm ($E_k$)** — Transforms plaintext into ciphertext using key $k$.
3. **Secret Key ($k \in \mathcal{K}$)** — Independent input that controls the transformation; must remain confidential.
4. **Ciphertext ($\mathcal{C}$)** — Scrambled, unreadable output $C = E_k(P)$.
5. **Decryption Algorithm ($D_k$)** — The mathematical inverse $D_k = E_k^{-1}$ that recovers the plaintext.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: State and explain Kerckhoff's Principle. Why is it important in modern cryptography?**

**Model Answer:**

**Kerckhoff's Principle** (formulated by Auguste Kerckhoffs in 1883) states:
> *"A cryptosystem should be secure even if everything about the system, except the key, is public knowledge."*

**Importance:**
- It forces designers to build **mathematically rigorous, publicly auditable** algorithms.
- It allows **widespread peer review** (e.g., AES was the result of an open NIST competition with 15 candidate algorithms).
- It enables **standardization and interoperability** across vendors.
- Algorithms that rely on "security by obscurity" (keeping the algorithm secret) are considered **inherently weak**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q: (a)** Draw the generalized model of a symmetric cipher and explain the roles of the plaintext, encryption algorithm, secret key, ciphertext, and decryption algorithm. Discuss the requirements that a secure symmetric encryption scheme must satisfy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of suitable diagrams and examples, explain the two classical techniques for building symmetric ciphers: **substitution** and **transposition**. Show that a Caesar cipher with shift $k$ is invertible, and compute the ciphertext of `"CRYPTOGRAPHY"` using $k = 7$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to A(a) — 7 Marks**

**1. Diagram (2 marks):** The schematic shown in **Section 4.1** above must be reproduced. Key labels: Alice (sender), Bob (receiver), plaintext $P$, encryption $E_k$, ciphertext $C$, decryption $D_k$, secret key $k$, secure channel, insecure channel.

**2. Role of Each Component (3 marks):**
- **Plaintext $P$:** The original intelligible message. It is the input that the legitimate parties want to keep confidential.
- **Encryption Algorithm $E_k$:** Performs mathematical transformations (substitution, permutation, modular arithmetic, etc.) on $P$ using $k$. The algorithm must be **deterministic** for any fixed $k$, and the mapping $E_k$ must be a **bijection** on $\mathcal{P}$.
- **Secret Key $k$:** An independent parameter selected from $\mathcal{K}$. Per **Kerckhoff's Principle**, the *security of the entire system rests on the secrecy of $k$*, not on the algorithm.
- **Ciphertext $C$:** The output $C = E_k(P)$. It must appear **random** to any third party who does not possess $k$.
- **Decryption Algorithm $D_k$:** The unique inverse of $E_k$, so that $D_k(C) = P$.

**3. Four Requirements for Secure Symmetric Encryption (2 marks):**
1. A **strong encryption algorithm** that resists cryptanalysis even when the algorithm is publicly known.
2. A **shared secret key** that the sender and receiver have agreed upon *prior* to communication.
3. A **secure key distribution mechanism** to deliver $k$ safely (e.g., out-of-band, KDC, or asymmetric-encrypted transport).
4. **Efficient implementation** — the algorithm should be fast enough for the target application domain (e.g., bulk encryption at gigabit speeds).

---

#### **Model Solution to A(b) — 7 Marks**

**1. Substitution Technique (2 marks):**
In substitution, each element of the plaintext (a letter, byte, or block) is **replaced** with another element drawn from a fixed or key-dependent alphabet. **Examples:** Caesar shift, monoalphabetic substitution tables, Vigenère (polyalphabetic). Substitution contributes **confusion** to the cipher (per Shannon).

**2. Transposition Technique (2 marks):**
In transposition, the plaintext characters are **rearranged (permuted)** in position, but the *symbol set itself* is unchanged. **Examples:** Rail Fence cipher, columnar transposition, route ciphers. Transposition contributes **diffusion**.

**3. Caesar Invertibility Proof (2 marks):**
For Caesar with shift $k$: $c = (p + k) \bmod 26$. The inverse is $p = (c - k) \bmod 26$.

$$ D_k(E_k(p)) = ((p + k) - k) \bmod 26 = p \bmod 26 = p \quad \checkmark $$

The map is a bijection because modular addition by a non-zero $k$ is invertible, and $k = 0$ (the identity map) is also invertible.

**4. Worked Numerical Computation (1 mark):**
Encrypt `"CRYPTOGRAPHY"` with $k = 7$:

$$ \begin{aligned}
\text{C}(2) &\to (2+7) \bmod 26 = 9 \to \text{J} \\
\text{R}(17) &\to (17+7) \bmod 26 = 24 \to \text{Y} \\
\text{Y}(24) &\to (24+7) \bmod 26 = 5 \to \text{F} \\
\text{P}(15) &\to (15+7) \bmod 26 = 22 \to \text{W} \\
\text{T}(19) &\to (19+7) \bmod 26 = 0 \to \text{A} \\
\text{O}(14) &\to (14+7) \bmod 26 = 21 \to \text{V} \\
\text{G}(6) &\to (6+7) \bmod 26 = 13 \to \text{N} \\
\text{R}(17) &\to 24 \to \text{Y} \\
\text{A}(0) &\to 7 \to \text{H} \\
\text{P}(15) &\to 22 \to \text{W} \\
\text{H}(7) &\to 14 \to \text{O} \\
\text{Y}(24) &\to 5 \to \text{F}
\end{aligned} $$

$$ C = \text{"JYFWAVNYHWOF"} $$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the three classical attack models in cryptanalysis: **ciphertext-only, known-plaintext, and chosen-plaintext attacks**. Rank them by the attacker's power and give one real-world example for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Vigenère cipher** with its mathematical formulation. Encrypt the plaintext `"NETWORK SECURITY"` using the keyword `"KEY"`, and show that the decryption correctly recovers the original message. Discuss the weakness of Vigenère and how **Kasiski examination** defeats it. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to B(a) — 7 Marks**

The three classical attack models, ranked from **weakest attacker** to **strongest attacker**:

**1. Ciphertext-Only Attack (COA) — Weakest (2 marks):**
- **Attacker's knowledge:** Several pieces of ciphertext encrypted with the same algorithm.
- **Attacker must deduce:** Either the key $k$ or the plaintexts $P_i$.
- **Method:** Exploit statistical properties of the language (letter frequencies, digram/trigram distributions, common words).
- **Real-world example:** Breaking a **monoalphabetic substitution cipher** by frequency analysis of a long intercepted message.

**2. Known-Plaintext Attack (KPA) — Intermediate (2.5 marks):**
- **Attacker's knowledge:** Several pairs of $(P_i, C_i)$ — i.e., both the plaintext and the resulting ciphertext.
- **Attacker must deduce:** The key $k$ (or an algorithm to decrypt *any* new ciphertext).
- **Method:** Use the known pairs to derive the key (e.g., XORing $P$ with $C$ directly reveals the key in a one-time pad mistake).
- **Real-world example:** WWII cryptanalysis of the **Enigma machine** relied partly on known plaintext "cribs" — the Germans often opened with predictable weather reports.

**3. Chosen-Plaintext Attack (CPA) — Strongest (2.5 marks):**
- **Attacker's knowledge:** The attacker can *temporarily* obtain the encryption oracle — submit plaintexts of their choosing and receive the corresponding ciphertexts.
- **Attacker must deduce:** The secret key.
- **Method:** Adaptive queries that probe the algorithm's internal structure; differential and linear cryptanalysis are practical CPA techniques.
- **Real-world example:** A **spy in a military communications unit** who can send arbitrary messages and observe the encrypted output.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B):** Students often confuse the *attacker's knowledge* with the *attacker's goal*. Remember: in all three cases, the ultimate goal is to recover the **key** (the most damaging) or the **plaintext** (one-shot). State both clearly. Marks are deducted for confusing COA with KPA — a common error worth 1–2 marks.

---

#### **Model Solution to B(b) — 7 Marks**

**1. Vigenère Cipher — Mathematical Model (2 marks):**

The Vigenère cipher uses a **repeating keyword** $K = k_1 k_2 \ldots k_m$ of length $m$. Let $K_i = K_{((i-1) \bmod m) + 1}$ be the key character at position $i$. Then:

$$ c_i = (p_i + K_i) \pmod{26} $$

$$ p_i = (c_i - K_i) \pmod{26} $$

The **keyspace** size is $\vert \mathcal{K} \vert = 26^m$.

**2. Encryption of `"NETWORK SECURITY"` with keyword `"KEY"` (2 marks):**

Keyword repetition: `KEYKEYKEYKEY K` (15 letters, matching plaintext).

| Plaintext | N | E | T | W | O | R | K | S | E | C | U | R | I | T | Y |
|-----------|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $p_i$ | 13 | 4 | 19 | 22 | 14 | 17 | 10 | 18 | 4 | 2 | 20 | 17 | 8 | 19 | 24 |
| Keyword | K | E | Y | K | E | Y | K | E | Y | K | E | Y | K | E | Y |
| $K_i$ | 10 | 4 | 24 | 10 | 4 | 24 | 10 | 4 | 24 | 10 | 4 | 24 | 10 | 4 | 24 |
| $c_i$ | 23 | 8 | 17 | 6 | 18 | 15 | 20 | 22 | 2 | 12 | 24 | 15 | 18 | 23 | 22 |
| Ciphertext | X | I | R | G | S | P | U | W | C | M | Y | P | S | X | W |

$$ C = \text{"XIRGSPUWC MYPSXW"} = \text{"XIRGSPUWC MYPSXW"} $$

**3. Decryption Verification (1.5 marks):**
Apply $p_i = (c_i - K_i) \bmod 26$:

$$ \begin{aligned}
X(23) - K(10) &= 13 \bmod 26 = 13 = N \quad \checkmark \\
I(8) - E(4) &= 4 = E \quad \checkmark \\
R(17) - Y(24) &= -7 \bmod 26 = 19 = T \quad \checkmark \\
G(6) - K(10) &= -4 \bmod 26 = 22 = W \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned} $$

All 15 letters recover correctly: $P = \text{"NETWORK SECURITY"}$.

**4. Weakness and Kasiski Examination (1.5 marks):**
- **Weakness:** Vigenère preserves a statistical "fingerprint" of the key length. When two identical plaintext letters fall under the *same* key letter (positions congruent mod $m$), they produce identical ciphertext letters.
- **Kasiski Examination (1863):** Find repeated **bigrams or trigrams** in the ciphertext (e.g., `"TH"`, `"HE"` appear frequently in English). Compute the GCD of the *distances* between repetitions — this GCD is overwhelmingly likely to be a multiple of the keyword length $m$. Once $m$ is known, the cipher reduces to $m$ independent Caesar ciphers, each crackable by frequency analysis.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part B - Vigenère):** A frequent mistake is forgetting to **mod 26** during subtraction. If the ciphertext letter is *numerically smaller* than the key letter, students get negative values. The correct procedure is $p_i = (c_i - K_i + 26) \bmod 26$. This mistake typically costs **1 full mark**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Symmetric Cipher Models**

- **5-tuple model:** $\mathcal{S} = (\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{C}, \mathcal{K}, E, D)$ with the identity $D_k(E_k(p)) = p$.
- **Kerckhoff's Principle:** Security rests on **key secrecy**, not algorithm secrecy.
- **Three classical attacks (weakest → strongest):** COA, KPA, CPA.
- **Four requirements for secure symmetric encryption:** Strong algorithm, shared secret key, secure key distribution, efficiency.
- **Shannon's two design principles:** *Confusion* (substitution) and *Diffusion* (transposition/permutation).
- **Two classical techniques:** *Substitution* (replace symbols) and *Transposition* (rearrange symbols).
- **Caesar cipher math:** $c = (p + k) \bmod 26$; $p = (c - k) \bmod 26$; keyspace $\vert \mathcal{K} \vert = 26$ (insecure).
- **Vigenère cipher math:** $c_i = (p_i + K_{i \bmod m}) \bmod 26$; keyspace $\vert \mathcal{K} \vert = 26^m$.
- **Brute-force formula:** $T_{\text{total}} = 2^{n-1} \times T_{\text{test}}$, where $n$ is the key length in bits.
- **Perfect secrecy (Shannon):** $H(K) \geq H(P)$ — only achieved by the one-time pad.
- **Frequency analysis** breaks any monoalphabetic substitution cipher with sufficient ciphertext length.
- **Kasiski examination** breaks Vigenère by recovering the keyword length via repeated bigram distances.
- **Modern ciphers** (DES, AES) use iterated **S-P networks** (alternating substitution and permutation rounds) to provide both confusion and diffusion.
- **Key takeaway for KTU exam:** Always state whether the algorithm is a *substitution*, *transposition*, or *product* cipher, and explicitly name the keyspace size.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Substitution Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET746 - CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Substitution Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Substitution Technique (Definition – KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Substitution Cipher** is a classical symmetric-key encryption method in which each element of the **plaintext** (typically a character or a block of characters) is systematically replaced by another element from a fixed substitution alphabet, drawn from a well-defined mathematical structure (group, ring, or finite field), to produce the **ciphertext**. The transformation is governed by an encryption function $E_k$ such that $C = E_k(P)$, and decryption is performed by the inverse mapping $P = D_k(C) = E_k^{-1}(C)$, where $k$ is the shared secret key.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, substitution techniques are formally classified under **classical symmetric-key cryptography**, which assumes a single shared key for both encryption and decryption, and the security rests entirely on the secrecy of the mapping rule (the *key*) rather than computational hardness assumptions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Classroom Seating Arrangement (Real-World Analogy):**
> Imagine a teacher has a fixed seating chart (the *key*). Every student in the class (the *plaintext* characters) is permanently assigned a unique seat number (the *ciphertext* symbols). To send a "secret message," the teacher simply writes down the seat numbers corresponding to the students' names in order. Anyone who knows the seating chart can instantly reconstruct the original message by reversing the lookup. The transformation is a **substitution**: one symbol standing in for another.

A simpler everyday example is the **ROT-13** trick used casually on internet forums, where every letter $A\dots Z$ is rotated 13 positions forward in the alphabet. Here, the underlying *substitution alphabet* is fixed and publicly known, but it still serves as the foundational model on which all subsequent classical ciphers are built.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Bold Constants & Metrics)

- **Plaintext ($P$):** The original, readable message in alphabet $\mathcal{P}$.
- **Ciphertext ($C$):** The encoded, unreadable message in alphabet $\mathcal{C}$.
- **Key Space ($\mathcal{K}$):** The set of all permissible keys; its **cardinality is $26!$** for arbitrary monoalphabetic substitution over the English alphabet.
- **Key Length ($|k|$):** The size of the secret key, typically measured in **bits**.
- **Alphabet Size ($|\Sigma|$):** For English letters, $|\Sigma| = 26$ (ignoring case and punctuation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:**
> The module explicitly tests three sub-families — **Caesar**, **monoalphabetic**, **polyalphabetic**, and the **Playfair**/**Hill** digraph-based systems. Mastery of the encryption and decryption algebraic forms is mandatory.

---

## 1.4 Classification Overview of Substitution Techniques

| Class | Mapping Property | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Simple (Monoalphabetic)** | One fixed ciphertext letter per plaintext letter | Caesar, Affine, Atbash |
| **Homophonic** | Multiple ciphertext symbols per plaintext letter | n-gram substitution |
| **Polyalphabetic** | Mapping changes with position using a key | Vigenère, Beaufort, Vernam |
| **Polygraphic (Digraph/Block)** | Operates on blocks of $n$ plaintext letters | Playfair ($n=2$), Hill ($n \geq 2$) |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Substitution Ciphers

The general substitution framework decomposes into the following logical pipeline:

1. **Define the source alphabet** $\mathcal{P} = \{a, b, c, \dots, z\}$ and map it to a numerical ring: $a \mapsto 0, b \mapsto 1, \dots, z \mapsto 25$. Formally, define a bijection $\phi : \mathcal{P} \to \mathbb{Z}_{26}$.
2. **Select a key** $k$ that parameterizes a permutation $\sigma_k$ on $\mathbb{Z}_{26}$.
3. **Encryption:** Apply the parameterized permutation to each numerical plaintext value: $C = \sigma_k(\phi(P))$.
4. **Decryption:** Apply the inverse permutation: $P = \phi^{-1}(\sigma_k^{-1}(C))$.
5. **Re-cast** the numerical ciphertext back into the target alphabet using $\phi^{-1}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why' Behind Modular Arithmetic:**
> The use of $\mathbb{Z}_{26}$ is not arbitrary. It reflects the fact that the English alphabet has exactly 26 letters, and modular arithmetic automatically enforces *wrap-around* (e.g., $25 + 3 = 28 \equiv 2 \pmod{26}$), guaranteeing that the ciphertext always remains inside the alphabet $\mathcal{P}$.

## 2.2 The Caesar Cipher

The Caesar cipher is the simplest monoalphabetic substitution. It shifts every letter by a fixed integer $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, 25\}$.

### Encryption Rule
For a plaintext character $p \in \mathbb{Z}_{26}$:

$$
E_k(p) = (p + k) \bmod 26
$$

### Decryption Rule
For a ciphertext character $c \in \mathbb{Z}_{26}$:

$$
D_k(c) = (c - k) \bmod 26
$$

### Properties
- **Key Space Size:** $\vert \mathcal{K} \vert = 26$ (only 25 non-trivial keys, since $k=0$ yields identity).
- **Vulnerability:** Trivially defeated by **brute-force search** in at most 25 attempts.
- **Historical Note:** Used by **Julius Caesar** with $k = 3$.

## 2.3 The Affine Cipher

The Affine cipher generalizes Caesar by using a linear function over $\mathbb{Z}_{26}$:

$$
E_k(p) = (a \cdot p + b) \bmod 26
$$

The key is the pair $k = (a, b)$ with the **essential constraint** that $\gcd(a, 26) = 1$, ensuring invertibility. The decryption uses the modular inverse $a^{-1} \pmod{26}$:

$$
D_k(c) = a^{-1} \cdot (c - b) \bmod 26
$$

### Key Space Size
The number of valid $a$ values is $\phi(26) = \phi(2) \cdot \phi(13) = 1 \cdot 12 = 12$. Therefore:

$$
\vert \mathcal{K} \vert = 12 \times 26 = 312
$$

## 2.4 The Playfair Cipher (Digraph Substitution)

The Playfair cipher encrypts **pairs of letters (digraphs)** using a $5 \times 5$ key square built from a keyword.

### Algorithm Summary
1. Construct a $5 \times 5$ matrix from the keyword (merging **I/J**).
2. Split plaintext into digraphs; insert a filler (typically **X**) on repeated letters and at the end.
3. For each digraph $(p_1, p_2)$:
   - **Same Row:** Shift each letter one position right (wrap-around).
   - **Same Column:** Shift each letter one position down (wrap-around).
   - **Rectangle:** Replace each letter with the one in its own row but in the column of the other letter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Space (Theoretical):** $26!$ permutations divided by symmetries. Effective key entropy is approximately **$2^{77.6}$ bits**, making brute force infeasible by hand — though modern cryptanalysis (frequency analysis on digraphs) breaks it quickly.

## 2.5 The Hill Cipher (Matrix-Based Polygraphic Substitution)

Invented by **Lester S. Hill (1929)**, this cipher works on blocks of $m$ letters using matrix multiplication over $\mathbb{Z}_{26}$.

### Encryption Rule
Let $P$ be an $m \times 1$ column vector of plaintext block values, and $K$ be an $m \times m$ invertible key matrix:

$$
C = K \cdot P \bmod 26
$$

### Decryption Rule
Let $K^{-1}$ be the modular inverse of $K$ modulo 26:

$$
P = K^{-1} \cdot C \bmod 26
$$

### Invertibility Condition
$\det(K) \bmod 26$ must be **coprime with 26** (i.e., $\gcd(\det K, 26) = 1$). Equivalently, $\det(K) \bmod 2 \neq 0$ and $\det(K) \bmod 13 \neq 0$.

## 2.6 The Vigenère Cipher (Polyalphabetic)

The Vigenère cipher uses a keyword of length $L$ and shifts each plaintext letter by a different amount depending on its position modulo $L$.

### Encryption Rule
Let keyword be $K = (k_0, k_1, \dots, k_{L-1})$ and plaintext index be $i$:

$$
E(p_i) = (p_i + k_{i \bmod L}) \bmod 26
$$

### Decryption Rule

$$
D(c_i) = (c_i - k_{i \bmod L}) \bmod 26
$$

### Key Space Size
For a keyword of length $L$, $\vert \mathcal{K} \vert = 26^L$.

## 2.7 The Vernam Cipher & One-Time Pad (OTP)

The Vernam cipher is a Vigenère cipher in which the key is **as long as the message** and used **once only**.

### Encryption Rule

$$
C_i = (P_i \oplus K_i)
$$

where $\oplus$ is the **bitwise XOR** (or mod-2 addition when reduced modulo 26).

### Shannon's Theorem
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shannon's Impossibility Theorem (1949):**
> The One-Time Pad provides **perfect secrecy**, meaning the ciphertext $C$ reveals *no* information about the plaintext $P$ in the information-theoretic sense: $H(P \mid C) = H(P)$. However, the key must be truly random, as long as the message, and never reused.

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| Cipher | Encryption Formula | Decryption Formula | Key Space $\vert \mathcal{K} \vert$ | Block Size |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Caesar | $C = (P + k) \bmod 26$ | $P = (C - k) \bmod 26$ | $25$ | $1$ |
| Affine | $C = (aP + b) \bmod 26$ | $P = a^{-1}(C - b) \bmod 26$ | $312$ | $1$ |
| Hill ($m \times m$) | $C = K P \bmod 26$ | $P = K^{-1} C \bmod 26$ | depends on $m$ | $m$ |
| Playfair | matrix-lookup rules | reverse matrix rules | $\approx 2^{77.6}$ | $2$ |
| Vigenère | $C_i = (P_i + K_{i \bmod L}) \bmod 26$ | $P_i = (C_i - K_{i \bmod L}) \bmod 26$ | $26^L$ | $1$ |
| Vernam (OTP) | $C_i = P_i \oplus K_i$ | $P_i = C_i \oplus K_i$ | unbounded | $1$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Although classical substitution ciphers are obsolete for security, they remain essential teaching tools and form the historical foundation of modern block ciphers like **DES** and **AES**, which use highly non-linear substitution boxes (S-boxes) inside Feistel/SPN structures.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Caesar Cipher Worked Example

**Problem:** Encrypt the plaintext `HELLO` using the Caesar cipher with key $k = 3$.

### Step 1 — Numerical Mapping ($\phi$)
Map each letter of `HELLO` to its index in $\mathbb{Z}_{26}$:

$$
H \mapsto 7, \quad E \mapsto 4, \quad L \mapsto 11, \quad L \mapsto 11, \quad O \mapsto 14
$$

### Step 2 — Apply Encryption Formula
For each index $p$, compute $(p + 3) \bmod 26$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H: \quad (7 + 3) \bmod 26 &= 10 \mapsto K \\
E: \quad (4 + 3) \bmod 26 &= 7 \mapsto H \\
L: \quad (11 + 3) \bmod 26 &= 14 \mapsto O \\
L: \quad (11 + 3) \bmod 26 &= 14 \mapsto O \\
O: \quad (14 + 3) \bmod 26 &= 17 \mapsto R
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Re-cast to Letters
Concatenate the result: `KHOOR`.

### Verification of Decryption
Apply $D_3(c) = (c - 3) \bmod 26$ to each ciphertext letter:

$$
K \mapsto 10 - 3 = 7 = H, \quad H \mapsto 4 = E, \quad O \mapsto 11 = L, \quad O \mapsto 11 = L, \quad R \mapsto 14 = O
$$

Result: `HELLO` ✓ (decryption is correct).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Affine Cipher Encryption

**Problem:** Encrypt `CAT` using Affine cipher with $a = 5, b = 8$.

### Step 1 — Numerical Mapping
$C \mapsto 2, A \mapsto 0, T \mapsto 19$.

### Step 2 — Validate Key
$\gcd(5, 26) = 1$ ✓ (key is valid, 5 is invertible mod 26).

### Step 3 — Apply Encryption $C = (5P + 8) \bmod 26$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C: \quad (5 \cdot 2 + 8) \bmod 26 &= (10 + 8) \bmod 26 = 18 \mapsto S \\
A: \quad (5 \cdot 0 + 8) \bmod 26 &= 8 \mapsto I \\
T: \quad (5 \cdot 19 + 8) \bmod 26 &= (95 + 8) \bmod 26 = 103 \bmod 26 = 103 - 3 \cdot 26 = 25 \mapsto Z
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Ciphertext
`SIZ`.

### Step 5 — Find Modular Inverse of $a$
Solve $5x \equiv 1 \pmod{26}$. Since $5 \cdot 21 = 105 = 4 \cdot 26 + 1$, we have $5^{-1} \equiv 21 \pmod{26}$.

### Step 6 — Decrypt to Verify
$P = 21(C - 8) \bmod 26$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S: \quad 21 \cdot (18 - 8) \bmod 26 &= 21 \cdot 10 \bmod 26 = 210 \bmod 26 = 210 - 8 \cdot 26 = 2 \mapsto C \\
I: \quad 21 \cdot (8 - 8) \bmod 26 &= 0 \mapsto A \\
Z: \quad 21 \cdot (25 - 8) \bmod 26 &= 21 \cdot 17 \bmod 26 = 357 \bmod 26 = 357 - 13 \cdot 26 = 19 \mapsto T
\end{aligned}
$$

Decryption yields `CAT` ✓.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Playfair Cipher Worked Example

**Setup:** Keyword = `MONARCHY`, Plaintext = `INSTRUMENTS`.

### Step 1 — Build the $5 \times 5$ Key Square
Fill row-by-row with unique letters of the keyword (treating I/J as one), then the remaining alphabet:

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
M & O & N & A & R \\
\hline
C & H & Y & B & D \\
\hline
E & F & G & I/J & K \\
\hline
L & P & Q & S & T \\
\hline
U & V & W & X & Z \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

### Step 2 — Preprocess Plaintext
`INSTRUMENTS` → split digraphs, inserting filler `X` on repeats: `IN ST RU ME NT SX` (last filler added for odd length).

### Step 3 — Encrypt Each Digraph
- `IN`: Rectangle — $I$ (row 2, col 3) → $G$ (row 2, col 2); $N$ (row 0, col 2) → $A$ (row 0, col 3). Cipher: `GA`.
- `ST`: Rectangle — $S$ (row 3, col 3) → $T$ (row 3, col 4); $T$ (row 3, col 4) → $S$ (row 3, col 3). Cipher: `TS`.
- `RU`: Rectangle — $R$ (row 0, col 4) → $K$ (row 2, col 4); $U$ (row 4, col 0) → $Z$ (row 4, col 4). Cipher: `KZ`.
- `ME`: Same column? $M$ (row 0, col 0), $E$ (row 2, col 0) → yes. Shift down: $M \to C$, $E \to L$. Cipher: `CL`.
- `NT`: Rectangle — $N$ (row 0, col 2) → $D$ (row 1, col 4); $T$ (row 3, col 4) → $R$ (row 0, col 4). Cipher: `DR`.
- `SX`: Same row? $S$ (row 3, col 3), $X$ (row 4, col 3) → no. Rectangle — $S$ → $X$, $X$ → $S$. Cipher: `XS`.

### Step 4 — Final Ciphertext
`GATSKCLDRXS`.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Hill Cipher ($m=2$)

**Problem:** Encrypt plaintext block `HI` using key matrix $K = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 3 \\ 2 & 5 \end{pmatrix}$.

### Step 1 — Numerical Mapping
$H \mapsto 7, I \mapsto 8$. So $P = \begin{pmatrix} 7 \\ 8 \end{pmatrix}$.

### Step 2 — Compute Determinant
$\det(K) = (3)(5) - (3)(2) = 15 - 6 = 9$. Since $\gcd(9, 26) = 1$ ✓, the matrix is invertible mod 26.

### Step 3 — Compute $C = K P \bmod 26$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 3 \\ 2 & 5 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 7 \\ 8 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \cdot 7 + 3 \cdot 8 \\ 2 \cdot 7 + 5 \cdot 8 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 21 + 24 \\ 14 + 40 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 45 \\ 54 \end{pmatrix} \\
C &\equiv \begin{pmatrix} 45 \bmod 26 \\ 54 \bmod 26 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 19 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} T \\ C \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Decrypt
First find $K^{-1} \bmod 26$. Since $\det(K) = 9$ and $9^{-1} \bmod 26$: solve $9x \equiv 1 \pmod{26}$. $9 \cdot 3 = 27 \equiv 1$, so $9^{-1} = 3$.

Adjugate of $K$ is $\begin{pmatrix} 5 & -3 \\ -2 & 3 \end{pmatrix} \equiv \begin{pmatrix} 5 & 23 \\ 24 & 3 \end{pmatrix} \pmod{26}$.

$$
K^{-1} = 9^{-1} \cdot \text{adj}(K) = 3 \cdot \begin{pmatrix} 5 & 23 \\ 24 & 3 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 15 & 69 \\ 72 & 9 \end{pmatrix} \equiv \begin{pmatrix} 15 & 17 \\ 20 & 9 \end{pmatrix} \pmod{26}
$$

Verify: $K^{-1} K = \begin{pmatrix} 15 \cdot 3 + 17 \cdot 2 & 15 \cdot 3 + 17 \cdot 5 \\ 20 \cdot 3 + 9 \cdot 2 & 20 \cdot 3 + 9 \cdot 5 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 79 & 130 \\ 78 & 105 \end{pmatrix} \equiv \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \pmod{26}$ ✓.

Now decrypt $C = \begin{pmatrix} 19 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= K^{-1} C = \begin{pmatrix} 15 & 17 \\ 20 & 9 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 19 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 15 \cdot 19 + 17 \cdot 2 \\ 20 \cdot 19 + 9 \cdot 2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 285 + 34 \\ 380 + 18 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 319 \\ 398 \end{pmatrix} \\
P &\equiv \begin{pmatrix} 319 \bmod 26 \\ 398 \bmod 26 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 7 \\ 8 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} H \\ I \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Decryption yields `HI` ✓.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Vigenère Cipher Example

**Setup:** Keyword = `KEY`, Plaintext = `ATTACKATDAWN`.

### Step 1 — Map Letters to Indices
Plaintext: A=0, T=19, T=19, A=0, C=2, K=10, A=0, T=19, D=3, A=0, W=22, N=13.
Key (repeating): K=10, E=4, Y=24, K=10, E=4, Y=24, K=10, E=4, Y=24, K=10, E=4, Y=24.

### Step 2 — Apply $C_i = (P_i + K_{i \bmod 3}) \bmod 26$

$$
\begin{aligned}
A: (0 + 10) \bmod 26 &= 10 \mapsto K \\
T: (19 + 4) \bmod 26 &= 23 \mapsto X \\
T: (19 + 24) \bmod 26 &= 17 \mapsto R \\
A: (0 + 10) \bmod 26 &= 10 \mapsto K \\
C: (2 + 4) \bmod 26 &= 6 \mapsto G \\
K: (10 + 24) \bmod 26 &= 8 \mapsto I \\
A: (0 + 10) \bmod 26 &= 10 \mapsto K \\
T: (19 + 4) \bmod 26 &= 23 \mapsto X \\
D: (3 + 24) \bmod 26 &= 1 \mapsto B \\
A: (0 + 10) \bmod 26 &= 10 \mapsto K \\
W: (22 + 4) \bmod 26 &= 0 \mapsto A \\
N: (13 + 24) \bmod 26 &= 11 \mapsto L
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Final Ciphertext
`KXRKGIKX BKAL` → `KXRKGIUKBKAL` (no space) — the canonical Vigenère test vector.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Strengths & Weaknesses

| Cipher | Key Length | Cryptanalysis Method | Key Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| Caesar | 5 bits | Brute force (25 tries) | Tiny key space |
| Affine | 9 bits | Brute force (312 tries) | Tiny key space |
| Playfair | $\approx 78$ bits | Digraph frequency | Same letter in pair fails differently |
| Hill ($m \geq 3$) | grows with $m^2$ | Known-plaintext attack | Vulnerable to linear algebra |
| Vigenère | $5L$ bits | **Kasiski test** / **Friedman test** | Key length recoverable |
| Vernam (OTP) | message length | None (perfect secrecy) | Key distribution impossible |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generalized Substitution Encryption/Decryption Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Plaintext Message P] --> B[Alphabet Mapping phi over Z26]
    B --> C[Select Key k from Key Space K]
    C --> D[Apply Encryption E sub k of P]
    D --> E[Numeric Ciphertext C in Z26]
    E --> F[Inverse Alphabet Mapping phi inverse]
    F --> G[Transmitted Ciphertext]
    H[Receiver obtains C] --> I[Compute D sub k of C]
    I --> J[Inverse Alphabet Map phi inverse]
    J --> K[Recovered Plaintext P]
    G --> H
```

## 4.2 Functional Hierarchy of Substitution Ciphers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Substitution Techniques] --> S1[Monoalphabetic]
    R --> S2[Polyalphabetic]
    R --> S3[Polygraphic Block]
    S1 --> A1[Caesar Cipher]
    S1 --> A2[Affine Cipher]
    S1 --> A3[Atbash Cipher]
    S2 --> B1[Vigenere Cipher]
    S2 --> B2[Beaufort Cipher]
    S2 --> B3[Vernam One Time Pad]
    S3 --> C1[Playfair Cipher m equals 2]
    S3 --> C2[Hill Cipher m greater than 2]
```

## 4.3 Cryptanalytic Attack Surface Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Adversary Intercepts Ciphertext] --> B[Step 1 Frequency Analysis]
    B --> C{Letter Distribution Flat}
    C -- Yes --> D[Possibly Polyalphabetic or OTP]
    C -- No --> E[Step 2 Index of Coincidence]
    E --> F{IC close to 0.066}
    F -- Yes --> G[Determine Key Length via Kasiski]
    F -- No --> H[Step 3 Digraph Frequency]
    H --> I[Target Playfair or Hill]
    G --> J[Recover Keyword]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Plaintext Recovered]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The IC (Index of Coincidence) value of **0.066** corresponds to a random English text distribution. Standard English has $\text{IC} \approx 0.065$, while uniform random has $\text{IC} \approx 0.038$. Comparing observed IC against these values is a primary statistical distinguisher.

## 4.4 Hill Cipher Operational Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Plaintext Vector P size m] --> M1[Multiply by Key Matrix K over Z26]
    M1 --> R1[Reduce Modulo 26]
    R1 --> C[Ciphertext Vector C size m]
    C --> M2[Multiply by Inverse Matrix K inverse over Z26]
    M2 --> R2[Reduce Modulo 26]
    R2 --> P2[Recovered Plaintext P]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> Differentiate between monoalphabetic and polyalphabetic ciphers with one example each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **monoalphabetic cipher** uses a **single fixed substitution alphabet** for all positions in the plaintext; the same plaintext letter always maps to the same ciphertext letter. Example: **Caesar cipher** with $k=3$ ($A \to D, B \to E, \dots$).
A **polyalphabetic cipher** uses **multiple substitution alphabets** that change with position according to a key, so the same plaintext letter can encrypt to different ciphertext letters at different positions. Example: **Vigenère cipher** with keyword `KEY`. Hence, polyalphabetic ciphers defeat simple frequency analysis, while monoalphabetic ones do not.

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> State **Shannon's theorem** on the One-Time Pad. Why is it not used in practice despite offering perfect secrecy?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Shannon's theorem (1949):** The One-Time Pad (Vernam cipher) achieves **perfect secrecy**, meaning $H(P \mid C) = H(P)$ — the ciphertext gives zero information about the plaintext in an information-theoretic sense.
**Why not in practice (3 reasons):**
1. The key must be **as long as the message**, making key generation expensive.
2. The key must be **truly random**, which is hard to guarantee.
3. The key can never be **reused**; secure distribution of such large keys is impractical.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question Choice A (14 Marks)

> **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1 Internal Choice]`**
> **(a)** Explain the **Caesar cipher** with an example. Encrypt the plaintext `SECURITY` using $k = 5$ and show the decryption process. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Describe the **Playfair cipher** algorithm. Using keyword `KEYWORD`, construct the $5 \times 5$ matrix and encrypt the digraph `HE LP`. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 marks]

The **Caesar cipher** is a monoalphabetic substitution in which each plaintext letter is shifted by a fixed key $k$ positions in the alphabet. Encryption: $C = (P + k) \bmod 26$. Decryption: $P = (C - k) \bmod 26$.

**Encrypt `SECURITY` with $k = 5$:**

Numerical map: $S=18, E=4, C=2, U=20, R=17, I=8, T=19, Y=24$.

Apply $(P + 5) \bmod 26$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S: (18+5) \bmod 26 &= 23 \mapsto X \\
E: (4+5) \bmod 26 &= 9 \mapsto J \\
C: (2+5) \bmod 26 &= 7 \mapsto H \\
U: (20+5) \bmod 26 &= 25 \mapsto Z \\
R: (17+5) \bmod 26 &= 22 \mapsto W \\
I: (8+5) \bmod 26 &= 13 \mapsto N \\
T: (19+5) \bmod 26 &= 24 \mapsto Y \\
Y: (24+5) \bmod 26 &= 3 \mapsto D
\end{aligned}
$$

**Ciphertext: `XJHZWNYD`.**

**Decrypt `XJHZWNYD`** with $k=5$: $P = (C-5) \bmod 26$ recovers `SECURITY` ✓.

### Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 marks]

The **Playfair cipher** encrypts digraphs using a $5 \times 5$ key matrix (I/J merged). Rules: (1) Same row → shift right; (2) Same column → shift down; (3) Rectangle → swap columns.

**Keyword = `KEYWORD`** → Key matrix:

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
K & E & Y & W & O \\
\hline
R & D & A & B & C \\
\hline
F & G & H & I/J & L \\
\hline
M & N & P & Q & S \\
\hline
T & U & V & X & Z \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Plaintext = `HELP`** → digraph: `HE` and `LP`.
- `HE`: $H$ is at (row 2, col 2), $E$ is at (row 0, col 1). Rectangle rule → $H \to D$ (row 2, col 1), $E \to B$ (row 0, col 2). **Digraph = `DB`.**
- `LP`: $L$ is at (row 2, col 4), $P$ is at (row 3, col 2). Rectangle rule → $L \to H$ (row 2, col 2), $P \to S$ (row 3, col 4). **Digraph = `HS`.**

**Final Ciphertext: `DBHS`.**

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks)

> **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1 Internal Choice]`**
> **(a)** With necessary equations, describe the **Affine cipher**. Given $a = 7, b = 3$, encrypt `DATA` and verify by decrypting the result. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Explain the **Hill cipher** with a $2 \times 2$ key matrix. Encrypt the plaintext block `DO` using $K = \begin{pmatrix} 5 & 8 \\ 1 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$ and find $K^{-1}$ to decrypt. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 marks]

The **Affine cipher** is a monoalphabetic substitution defined by $E(p) = (ap + b) \bmod 26$, where $\gcd(a, 26) = 1$ is required for invertibility. Decryption: $D(c) = a^{-1}(c - b) \bmod 26$.

Given $a = 7, b = 3$. Numerical map of `DATA`: $D=3, A=0, T=19, A=0$.

Compute modular inverse of $7$: $7 \cdot 15 = 105 = 4 \cdot 26 + 1$, so $7^{-1} = 15 \pmod{26}$.

**Encryption** $C = (7P + 3) \bmod 26$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D: (7 \cdot 3 + 3) \bmod 26 &= 24 \mapsto Y \\
A: (7 \cdot 0 + 3) \bmod 26 &= 3 \mapsto D \\
T: (7 \cdot 19 + 3) \bmod 26 &= 136 \bmod 26 = 6 \mapsto G \\
A: 3 \bmod 26 &= 3 \mapsto D
\end{aligned}
$$

**Ciphertext: `YDGD`.**

**Decryption** $P = 15(C - 3) \bmod 26$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y: 15 \cdot 21 \bmod 26 &= 315 \bmod 26 = 3 \mapsto D \\
D: 15 \cdot 0 \bmod 26 &= 0 \mapsto A \\
G: 15 \cdot 3 \bmod 26 &= 45 \bmod 26 = 19 \mapsto T \\
D: 0 \mapsto A
\end{aligned}
$$

**Recovered: `DATA` ✓.**

### Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 marks]

The **Hill cipher** encrypts blocks of $m$ letters using $C = K P \bmod 26$, where $K$ is an $m \times m$ invertible key matrix. Decryption: $P = K^{-1} C \bmod 26$.

**Given:** $K = \begin{pmatrix} 5 & 8 \\ 1 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$, plaintext `DO` → $P = \begin{pmatrix} D \\ O \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 14 \end{pmatrix}$.

**Step 1 — Determinant:** $\det(K) = 5 \cdot 3 - 8 \cdot 1 = 15 - 8 = 7$. Since $\gcd(7, 26) = 1$ ✓, the matrix is invertible.

**Step 2 — Compute $C$:**

$$
C = \begin{pmatrix} 5 & 8 \\ 1 & 3 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 14 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 15 + 112 \\ 3 + 42 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 127 \\ 45 \end{pmatrix} \equiv \begin{pmatrix} 127 - 4 \cdot 26 \\ 45 - 1 \cdot 26 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 23 \\ 19 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} X \\ T \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Ciphertext: `XT`.**

**Step 3 — Find $K^{-1}$:** $7^{-1} \bmod 26$: $7 \cdot 15 = 105 \equiv 1$, so $7^{-1} = 15$.

$K^{-1} = 15 \cdot \begin{pmatrix} 3 & -8 \\ -1 & 5 \end{pmatrix} = 15 \cdot \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 18 \\ 25 & 5 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 45 & 270 \\ 375 & 75 \end{pmatrix} \equiv \begin{pmatrix} 19 & 10 \\ 11 & 23 \end{pmatrix} \pmod{26}$.

**Step 4 — Decrypt to Verify:**

$$
P = \begin{pmatrix} 19 & 10 \\ 11 & 23 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 23 \\ 19 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 437 + 190 \\ 253 + 437 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 627 \\ 690 \end{pmatrix} \equiv \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 14 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} D \\ O \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Recovered: `DO` ✓.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Numerical indexing error:** Using $A=1$ instead of $A=0$ leads to all subsequent shifts being off by 1, causing the entire answer to be marked wrong. **Always explicitly state $A \mapsto 0$.**
> 2. **Forgetting the filler in Playfair:** When splitting digraphs, students often forget to insert the `X` filler on repeated letters. The examiner specifically checks for this; missing it loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Hill cipher invertibility:** Students frequently skip the $\gcd(\det K, 26) = 1$ check, leading to decryption failure in viva. Always verify.
> 4. **Vigenère key alignment:** The keyword must be cycled starting from the *first* plaintext letter, not after spaces. Removing spaces before encryption is a common error.
> 5. **Modular wrap-around:** In Caesar/Affine, students often compute $(p+k)$ without taking mod 26, producing out-of-range values like 28 instead of 2. Show the mod operation explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist — Substitution Techniques**
> - **Caesar:** Single shift $k$; key space = **25**; trivially breakable.
> - **Affine:** $C = aP + b$; requires $\gcd(a, 26) = 1$; key space = **312**.
> - **Playfair:** Digraph-based; $5 \times 5$ matrix; rules = same-row / same-column / rectangle; merges I/J; uses filler `X` on doubles.
> - **Hill:** Block cipher; matrix multiplication mod 26; invertibility needs $\gcd(\det K, 26) = 1$.
> - **Vigenère:** Polyalphabetic; keyword cycles; defeats single-letter frequency analysis.
> - **Vernam (OTP):** Key as long as message, never reused; gives **perfect secrecy** (Shannon, 1949).
> - **Key takeaway:** Classical ciphers are **information-theoretically weak** — broken by frequency analysis, Kasiski test, Index of Coincidence, and brute force. They are stepping stones to **modern symmetric-key ciphers (DES, AES)**.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET746 - CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1: TRANSPOSITION TECHNIQUES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)**
> *A **Transposition Cipher** is a classical symmetric-key encryption technique in which the **letters of the plaintext are permuted (rearranged) without substituting or changing the original characters themselves**. The permutation is governed by a secret key, and the same key is required to reverse the permutation during decryption. The original statistical letter-frequency distribution of the plaintext language is **preserved** in the ciphertext, which is the hallmark of any transposition system.*

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Locked Anagram"

Imagine you write a sensitive sentence — say, **"CALL ME TOMORROW"** — on a strip of paper, then cut the strip into small squares (one letter per square) and stuff them into a sealed envelope **without changing any letter**. The letters are intact, but the order is scrambled. The receiver, who knows the **un-scrambling key** (e.g., *put letters in odd positions first, then even positions*), can re-arrange them back into the original sentence.

In short:

> **Substitution** = *Change what each letter is.*
> **Transposition** = *Change where each letter goes.*

The plaintext *characters* are identical to the ciphertext *characters*; only their **positions** differ. This is why transposition ciphers are also called **permutation ciphers**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Examinations**
> - **Substitution Ciphers** alter the **identity** of letters (e.g., Caesar, Playfair, Vigenère).
> - **Transposition Ciphers** alter the **position** of letters (e.g., Rail Fence, Columnar).
> - A **Product Cipher** combines both (e.g., DES, AES in modern systems).

### 1.1 The Classical Symmetric Cipher Model (Context)

All classical ciphers (substitution and transposition) operate under the symmetric-key model:

$$
C = E_k(P) \quad \text{and} \quad P = D_k(C)
$$

where $P$ is plaintext, $C$ is ciphertext, $E_k$ is the encryption function under key $k$, and $D_k$ is the corresponding decryption function. For classical transposition ciphers, the key $k$ is the **permutation rule** (e.g., number of rails, or a numeric key word).

### 1.2 Classification of Transposition Ciphers

Transposition ciphers are classified along two axes:

| Axis | Type 1 | Type 2 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **By Key Type** | Keyless (fixed structure) | Keyed (variable permutation) |
| **By Unit** | Route Ciphers (entire block) | Scytale-type (geometric wrap) |

**The five KTU-relevant techniques** are:

1. **The Scytale Cipher** (historical, keyless geometric)
2. **Rail Fence Cipher** (keyless depth-based)
3. **Simple Columnar Transposition** (fixed number of columns)
4. **Columnar Transposition with Key Word** (keyed)
5. **Double Transposition** (keyed, applied twice)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Scytale Cipher (Spartan, ~500 BC)

The Scytale was the earliest known military transposition device used by Spartan commanders.

- A strip of parchment is wound helically around a cylindrical rod of fixed **diameter**.
- The message is written along the axis of the rod.
- When unwound, the letters appear in a scrambled order.
- The **diameter of the rod is the key**.

**Encryption (intuitive model):**
- Plaintext is written *row by row* into a matrix of width $w$ (equal to the rod's circumference).
- Ciphertext is read *column by column*.

> [!NOTE]
> The Scytale is fundamentally equivalent to a **simple columnar transposition** with $w$ columns.

### 2.2 The Rail Fence Cipher

The plaintext is written along a **zig-zag pattern** across $d$ rows (the **depth** $d$ is the key), then read row by row.

- **Encryption:** Write the plaintext diagonally down-up-down-up… across $d$ rails, then read each row left to right.
- **Decryption:** Know the depth $d$ and length $n$, then reconstruct the zig-zag and read diagonally.

**Key parameter:** the depth $d \geq 2$.

**For depth $d$, the row-lengths of the zig-zag are governed by:**

$$
L_{\text{top}} = \lceil n / (2d - 2) \rceil \quad \text{when } n \text{ is large and } d \geq 3
$$

For $d=2$, exactly $n/2$ letters fall on each rail (when $n$ is even).

### 2.3 Simple Columnar Transposition

The plaintext is written into a matrix of **$c$ columns** (where $c$ is the key) row by row, and the ciphertext is read column by column.

- **Encryption:** Fill a matrix of width $c$ row by row; read column 1 top-to-bottom, then column 2, …, column $c$.
- **Padding:** If plaintext length $n$ is not a multiple of $c$, the last row is padded with arbitrary characters (often `X`).
- **Decryption:** Know $c$, compute the number of full columns $\lfloor n/c \rfloor$ and short columns, then fill column by column and read row by row.

### 2.4 Keyed Columnar Transposition (Most Common Exam Variant)

This is the **workhorse of classical transposition** and the most-tested technique in KTU exams.

**Procedure:**
1. Choose a numerical key of length $m$ (typically derived from a key word by ranking letters alphabetically).
2. Write the plaintext into a matrix with $m$ columns row by row.
3. Read the columns **in the alphabetical order of the key letters** (or equivalently, the ascending numerical order of the key digits).
4. The receiver uses the same key to reconstruct the matrix column by column in the correct order, then reads row by row.

**Example of key-derivation:**
Key word `ZEBRAS` → letter ranks:

| Letter | Z | E | B | R | A | S |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Alphabetical Rank | 6 | 3 | 2 | 4 | 1 | 5 |

So the **read order** is column 5, then column 3, then column 2, then column 4, then column 6, then column 1.

### 2.5 Double Transposition

Two keyed columnar transpositions are applied sequentially with **two different keys** $k_1$ and $k_2$ (often $k_1 = k_2$ is also permitted). This dramatically increases security — equivalent work factor becomes $O(m^2)$ for key length $m$.

### 2.6 Cryptanalysis of Transposition Ciphers

Because transposition preserves letter frequencies, the **Index of Coincidence (IC)** is the principal detection tool.

$$
IC = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{26} f_i (f_i - 1)}{N (N - 1)}
$$

where $f_i$ is the frequency of the $i$-th letter in the ciphertext, and $N$ is the total number of letters.

**Diagnostic IC values (English language):**

| Cipher Type | Expected IC |
| :--- | :--- |
| Plain English text | $\approx 0.065$ |
| Monoalphabetic substitution | $\approx 0.065$ |
| Polyalphabetic / random | $\approx 0.038$ |
| **Transposition (any)** | **$\approx 0.065$** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb:** If the IC of a ciphertext is $\approx 0.065$, the cipher is either a simple substitution **or a transposition**. To distinguish, perform a **Kasiski / Friedman test** on repeated digram/trigram distances, or look for a "columnar" structure in the ciphertext by trying various column counts.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Rule | Notes / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Symmetric Encryption | $C = E_k(P)$ | Classical model |
| 2 | Rail Fence Depth $d$ | $d \in \mathbb{Z}^+,\ d \geq 2$ | $d=1$ is identity |
| 3 | Columnar Matrix Width | $c = $ number of columns | Key for simple columnar |
| 4 | Number of Full Rows | $r = \lfloor n/c \rfloor$ | $n$ = plaintext length |
| 5 | Number of Short Cells | $n \bmod c$ | Cells in the last partial row |
| 6 | Index of Coincidence | $IC = \frac{\sum_i f_i (f_i-1)}{N(N-1)}$ | English $\approx 0.065$ |
| 7 | Expected IC (random) | $IC_{\text{rand}} = 1/26 \approx 0.0385$ | Uniform distribution |
| 8 | Key-Rank Mapping | Rank letters alphabetically; ties broken left-to-right | ZEBRAS $\to$ 6 3 2 4 1 5 |
| 9 | Double Transposition Work | Two independent keys $k_1, k_2$ of length $m$ | $\binom{m}{}$ permutations possible |

**Real-world utility:** Transposition ciphers are no longer used alone for security (defeated by frequency analysis + IC tests), but the **principle of permutation (S-P networks, bit permutations in DES, byte shuffling in AES ShiftRows)** is foundational to every modern block cipher. Understanding classical transposition is the conceptual gateway to understanding the diffusion property of Shannon's principles of cryptography.
