## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A, Q1(a)]**
> **Q1. Define a *Primitive Operation* in cryptography. List the four primary families with one example use of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *primitive operation* is an elementary, atomic mathematical or logical operation that serves as a fundamental building block of cryptographic algorithms. The four primary families are:
1. **Substitution (S-operation):** e.g., AES SubBytes using a 16×16 S-box for confusion.
2. **Permutation (P-operation):** e.g., AES ShiftRows for diffusion.
3. **Modular Arithmetic operations:** e.g., Modular exponentiation $a^{b} \bmod n$ used in RSA.
4. **Bitwise Logical operations:** e.g., XOR used in stream ciphers and the AddRoundKey step of AES.

*(Award ½ mark for definition + ½ mark per family with example.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Supplementary, Q1(b)]**
> **Q2. Why is the XOR operation the most widely used primitive in symmetric cryptography? State two reasons.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Self-inverse property:** $A \oplus B \oplus B = A$. The *same* operation is used for both encryption and decryption — this halves the implementation cost. *(1.5 marks)*
2. **Bitwise independence & speed:** Each bit of the output depends on only one bit of each input, allowing trivial hardware implementation in 4-MOSFET XOR gates, and giving constant-time execution (side-channel resistant). *(1.5 marks)*

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Main, Q2 (Module 2)]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **Square-and-Multiply** algorithm for fast modular exponentiation. Compute $5^{29} \bmod 23$ using this method, showing every intermediate step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the role of **Substitution, Permutation, XOR, and Modular Addition** as primitive operations inside the AES round function. Explain how *confusion* and *diffusion* are achieved. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Square-and-Multiply Algorithm & Computation:**

**Algorithm (right-to-left binary form):**
1. Express exponent $b$ in binary.
2. Set result $R = 1$.
3. Scan bits of $b$ from MSB to LSB; for each bit, **square** $R$ (mod $n$), and if the bit is 1, **multiply** $R$ by the base (mod $n$).

**Computing $5^{29} \bmod 23$:**

$29 = (11101)_2 = 16 + 8 + 4 + 1$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= 1 \\
\text{Bit 4 = 1:} \quad R &= 1^2 \cdot 5 = 5 \bmod 23 \quad &\Rightarrow R = 5 \\
\text{Bit 3 = 1:} \quad R &= 5^2 \cdot 5 = 25 \cdot 5 = 125 \bmod 23 \\
    &= (5 \cdot 23 + 10) = 125 - 115 = 10 \quad &\Rightarrow R = 10 \\
\text{Bit 2 = 1:} \quad R &= 10^2 \cdot 5 = 100 \cdot 5 = 500 \bmod 23 \\
    &= (21 \cdot 23 + 17) = 500 - 483 = 17 \quad &\Rightarrow R = 17 \\
\text{Bit 1 = 0:} \quad R &= 17^2 = 289 \bmod 23 \\
    &= (12 \cdot 23 + 13) = 289 - 276 = 13 \quad &\Rightarrow R = 13 \\
\text{Bit 0 = 1:} \quad R &= 13^2 \cdot 5 = 169 \cdot 5 = 845 \bmod 23 \\
    &= (36 \cdot 23 + 17) = 845 - 828 = 17 \quad &\Rightarrow R = 17
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{5^{29} \bmod 23 = 17}$$

*(4 marks for algorithm + 3 marks for correct trace.)*

**(b) AES Round Function — Primitive Operations (refer to Mermaid diagram in SECTION 4.3):**

The AES round function chains four primitive operations on a 128-bit state:

1. **SubBytes (Substitution):** A non-linear byte-wise lookup in a 16×16 S-box derived from inversion in $\mathrm{GF}(2^{8})$ followed by an affine transform. This provides **confusion** — the algebraic relationship between key and ciphertext becomes non-linear, defeating linear and differential cryptanalysis. *(2 marks)*

2. **ShiftRows (Permutation):** Row $i$ of the 4×4 state matrix is cyclically shifted left by $i$ byte positions. This is a deterministic position bijection providing **diffusion** — the influence of a single plaintext byte spreads across the entire 128-bit state over multiple rounds. *(1.5 marks)*

3. **MixColumns (Modular Matrix Multiplication):** Each 4-byte column is multiplied by a fixed $4 \times 4$ MDS matrix over $\mathrm{GF}(2^{8})$. This is a *modular arithmetic* primitive (polynomial multiplication modulo $x^{4} + 1$). It propagates the diffusion from byte-level (ShiftRows) to bit-level. *(1.5 marks)*

4. **AddRoundKey (XOR):** A bitwise XOR of the state with a 128-bit round key derived from the key schedule. This injects *key material* and is the *only* operation that depends on the key. *(1 mark)*

**Confusion–Diffusion Achievement:**
- *Confusion* comes from the non-linear S-box.
- *Diffusion* comes from ShiftRows + MixColumns.
- *Key coupling* comes from AddRoundKey (XOR).
- A single AES round provides both properties; 10/12/14 rounds (for 128/192/256-bit keys) ensure cryptanalytic strength. *(1 mark)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE**

**(a)** Using the **Extended Euclidean Algorithm**, find the modular inverse of $17 \bmod 53$. Verify the answer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What are the design criteria of a cryptographically strong **S-box**? List any three properties, and contrast the **substitution** primitive with the **permutation** primitive in terms of confusion versus diffusion contribution. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Finding $17^{-1} \bmod 53$ via Extended Euclidean Algorithm:**

**Step 1 — Euclidean Division Sequence:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
53 &= 17 \cdot 3 + 2 \\
17 &= 2 \cdot 8 + 1 \\
2 &= 1 \cdot 2 + 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, $\gcd(17, 53) = 1$ (inverse exists).

**Step 2 — Extended Euclidean Back-Substitution:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
1 &= 17 - 2 \cdot 8 \\
  &= 17 - (53 - 17 \cdot 3) \cdot 8 \\
  &= 17 \cdot 25 - 53 \cdot 8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Read off the inverse:**
$$17 \cdot 25 - 53 \cdot 8 = 1 \implies 17 \cdot 25 \equiv 1 \pmod{53}$$

$$\boxed{17^{-1} \bmod 53 = 25}$$

**Step 4 — Verification:**
$$17 \times 25 = 425 = 8 \times 53 + 1 = 424 + 1 \equiv 1 \pmod{53} \quad \checkmark$$

*(2 marks Euclidean step, 3 marks back-substitution, 1 mark verification, 1 mark final answer.)*

**(b) S-Box Design Criteria & S-vs-P Contrast:**

**Three essential S-box design properties (KTU/NIST standard answer):**

1. **Bijection (Permutation property):** Every output value must have a unique pre-image, ensuring invertibility for decryption. *(1 mark)*

2. **Non-linearity:** The S-box must not be expressible as a linear function over $\mathrm{GF}(2)^n$ (high *non-linear order*). This blocks **linear cryptanalysis** by Matsui (1993) and ensures the cipher resists **differential cryptanalysis** (Biham-Shamir). *(1 mark)*

3. **Strict Avalanche Criterion (SAC):** Flipping any single input bit must change *exactly 50%* of the output bits on average. This is the formal measure of *avalanche* and is essential for *diffusion* per bit. *(1 mark)*

**Additional accepted criteria:**
- **Bit Independence Criterion (BIC):** Output bits must change independently.
- **Algebraic complexity:** The S-box should not be representable as a low-degree polynomial over $\mathrm{GF}(2^n)$ (this defeats interpolation attacks).
- **No fixed points / no opposite fixed points:** $S(x) \neq x$ and $S(x) \neq \overline{x}$ for all $x$.

**Substitution vs. Permutation — Confusion vs. Diffusion:**

| Property | Substitution (S-box) | Permutation (P-box) |
|---|---|---|
| Mathematical nature | Value-changing bijection (lookup) | Position-rearranging bijection |
| Primary role | **Confusion** (Shannon) | **Diffusion** (Shannon) |
| Output bit values | New values; non-linear in inputs | Identical values, just re-ordered |
| Reversible? | Yes (must be bijective) | Yes (always bijective) |
| Provides non-linearity? | **Yes** — essential | No — purely linear over bit positions |
| Example | AES SubBytes, DES S-boxes | AES ShiftRows, DES Initial Permutation |
| Cryptanalytic threat if weak | Linear & differential cryptanalysis | Bit-pattern / slide attacks |

*(1 mark for the contrast table, 1 mark for example ciphers.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusion vs. Diffusion mix-up:** A shocking number of students *swap* the roles — S-box gives confusion, P-box gives diffusion. **Memorize this distinction cold.**
> 2. **Missing the inverse verification step** in modular arithmetic: KTU examiners explicitly award the final 1 mark only if you *multiply back* to confirm.
> 3. **Skipping intermediate modular reduction** in Square-and-Multiply: Show *every* square and *every* multiply with the mod. Writing $5^2 = 25 \bmod 23 = 2$ is **far better** than jumping to the final answer.
> 4. **Confusing "modular exponentiation" with "modular multiplication"** in the algorithm complexity — modular exponentiation costs $O((\log n)^3)$, not $O((\log n)^2)$.
> 5. **Forgetting that XOR is self-inverse:** Many students write "decryption uses NOT-XOR" — this is **wrong**, decryption uses *the same XOR* with the same key.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **Primitive Operation** is an *atomic* cryptographic building block: Substitution, Permutation, Modular Arithmetic, or Bitwise Logical.
- ✅ **S-box (Substitution) = Confusion**; **P-box (Permutation) = Diffusion** — *this single sentence has earned 8+ marks in past KTU papers.*
- ✅ **XOR** is the workhorse of symmetric cryptography because it is *self-inverse* and *bit-independent* (fast, side-channel resistant).
- ✅ **Modular exponentiation** $a^{b} \bmod n$ is the *expensive* primitive that powers RSA, DH, and ElGamal — complexity $O((\log n)^{3})$.
- ✅ **Square-and-Multiply** converts $O(n)$ multiplications into $O(\log b)$ multiplications — **the** algorithm to memorize.
- ✅ **Modular inverse** exists iff $\gcd(a, n) = 1$, computed via the **Extended Euclidean Algorithm** in $O((\log n)^{2})$.
- ✅ AES uses **four primitive operations** in a round: SubBytes, ShiftRows, MixColumns, AddRoundKey.
- ✅ DES uses **Substitution + Permutation + XOR** (a classic *Feistel* structure).
- ✅ The **Avalanche Effect** is the key qualitative property of good primitive operations — flip 1 bit in input, change *roughly half* of all output bits.
- ✅ Primitive operations are *constant-time* to implement in hardware to prevent **timing side-channel attacks**.
- ✅ **Post-quantum** cryptography replaces modular exponentiation with *polynomial modular multiplication* over lattices.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Line Mnemonic for the KTU Board Exam:**
> ***"**S**ubstitution brings **S**hannon's **C**onfusion; **P**ermutation provides **P**ervasive **D**iffusion; **XOR** **X**-changes the bits reversibly; **Modular Exp** makes **E**very public-key scheme work."***
>
> Memorize this sentence — it has appeared (in different phrasings) in 4 out of 5 recent KTU Cryptography papers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inverse Cipher - Key Expansion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET746 - CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inverse Cipher - Key Expansion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔐 Inverse Cipher – Key Expansion (AES Decryption Key Schedule)

## 1. Core Technical Definition

### 📘 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the **Advanced Encryption Standard (AES)** algorithm, the **Inverse Cipher** is the mathematical inverse of the Forward Cipher. While the Forward Cipher uses the round keys in the *natural* order of generation, the **Inverse Cipher applies the same round keys in REVERSE order**, and the round functions (SubBytes, ShiftRows, MixColumns) are replaced by their inverses (InvSubBytes, InvShiftRows, InvMixColumns).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Expansion for the Inverse Cipher** is the procedure used to **derive the round keys required by the decryption process**. For **AES-128**, the encryption key schedule is reused as-is (only the order of application is reversed). For **AES-192** and **AES-256**, the encryption key schedule is *post-processed* by applying the **InvMixColumns** transformation to a subset of the round keys, so that InvMixColumns can be cancelled out during decryption — yielding a key schedule that works cleanly with the inverse round functions.

### 🧠 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine a **one-way turnstile** in a metro station. You insert a token and pass through in one direction (Forward Cipher). To exit, you cannot use the same turnstile in the same way — you need a **different mechanism (Inverse Cipher)** that *undoes* the effect of entry.

In AES:
- **Forward Cipher** → Encrypts plaintext using round keys K₀, K₁, K₂, ..., K_{N_r}
- **Inverse Cipher** → Decrypts ciphertext using round keys K_{N_r}, K_{N_r−1}, ..., K₁, K₀ (in **reverse order**)

Now, the *Key Expansion* algorithm generates these round keys. For **AES-128**, the same key expansion output works perfectly for both encryption and decryption (just read backwards). But for **AES-192 and AES-256**, the order of round-function application interacts with the key application in a way that requires a special adjustment — applying **InvMixColumns** to certain intermediate round keys.

> [!NOTE]
> **Metric Reference:** AES supports three standard key sizes — **128, 192, and 256 bits** — corresponding to **10, 12, and 14 rounds (N_r)** respectively, with **N_k = 4, 6, 8** key words (32 bits each).

---

## 2. Why Does the Inverse Cipher Need a Special Key Schedule?

### 🔍 The Core Problem

In the **Forward Cipher**, the final round omits MixColumns. Therefore:
- The last application of MixColumns occurs in **round (N_r − 1)**
- During decryption, **InvMixColumns** is applied in **round 1** of the Inverse Cipher

When using a single, shared key schedule:
- The **last** round key used in encryption (K_{N_r}) and the **first** round key used in decryption (also K_{N_r}) are the same
- The InvMixColumns applied in decryption round 1 must be **cancelled out** by an equivalent operation embedded in the decryption key schedule

This is achieved by **pre-multiplying** the affected round keys by the **InvMixColumns matrix** in GF(2⁸), which algebraically cancels the extra InvMixColumns operation.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** This is a classic application of the algebraic property — if a transformation **T** is applied to a key, and the inverse transformation **T⁻¹** is applied to data, the net effect is identity (T⁻¹ · T = I). AES decryption leverages this to make its structure mirror encryption.

---

## 3. Notation Used Throughout the Module

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| $N_b$ | Block length in 32-bit words (always **4** for AES) |
| $N_k$ | Key length in 32-bit words (**4, 6, 8** for 128/192/256-bit keys) |
| $N_r$ | Number of rounds (**10, 12, 14** for 128/192/256-bit keys) |
| $w[i]$ | The $i$-th 32-bit word of the expanded key |
| $K_i$ | Round key for round $i$ (consists of $N_b = 4$ words) |
| $\oplus$ | Bitwise XOR |
| $\text{SubWord}()$ | Apply S-box to each of the 4 bytes of a word |
| $\text{RotWord}()$ | Cyclic left rotation of a word by 1 byte |
| $\text{Rcon}[j]$ | Round constant for round $j$ |
| $\text{InvMixColumns}()$ | The 4-byte transformation equivalent to the inverse MixColumns matrix |

---

## 4. Summary of Encryption vs. Decryption Key Schedule

| AES Variant | Encryption Key Schedule | Decryption (Inverse) Key Schedule |
|-------------|------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| **AES-128** | Standard expansion | **Same** as encryption; round keys applied in reverse |
| **AES-192** | Standard expansion | Encryption key schedule with **InvMixColumns** applied to **round keys 1 through N_r − 1** |
| **AES-256** | Standard expansion | Encryption key schedule with **InvMixColumns** applied to **round keys 1, 3, 5, ..., N_r − 1** (the odd-numbered round keys) |

---

## 5. Conceptual Recap (Section 1)

- The **Inverse Cipher** decrypts AES ciphertext using the **same round keys** generated by the AES key expansion, but in **reverse order**.
- For **AES-128**, no modification to the key schedule is needed.
- For **AES-192 and AES-256**, the key schedule must be **modified** by applying **InvMixColumns** to specific round keys.
- This adjustment is necessary to **cancel out** the extra InvMixColumns operation in the first round of the inverse cipher.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📚 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 1. Review: Encryption Key Expansion (Forward Cipher)

The encryption key expansion takes the original cipher key of $N_k$ words and expands it into a linear array of $N_b \cdot (N_r + 1)$ words, denoted $w[0], w[1], \ldots, w[N_b \cdot (N_r + 1) - 1]$.

### 🔧 Pseudo-code (Algorithm Description, Not Code)

The key expansion uses the following **logic steps** for AES-128 and AES-192:

**For $i = 0$ to $N_k - 1$ (and $N_k - 1$ for AES-256):**
$$w[i] = \text{key}[i]$$
(Initial key words are copied directly from the cipher key)

**For $i = N_k$ to $N_b(N_r + 1) - 1$:**

$$w[i] = w[i - N_k] \oplus w[i - 1] \quad \text{if } i \text{ is NOT a multiple of } N_k$$

$$w[i] = w[i - N_k] \oplus \text{SubWord}(\text{RotWord}(w[i-1])) \oplus \text{Rcon}[i/N_k] \quad \text{if } i \text{ is a multiple of } N_k$$

**For AES-256 ONLY, an additional step** applies when $i \equiv 4 \pmod{N_k}$ (i.e., $i = 4, 12, 20, 28, 36, 44$):

$$w[i] = w[i - N_k] \oplus \text{SubWord}(w[i-1])$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the AES-256 modification?** This extra SubWord step increases the non-linearity of the key schedule for the longer 256-bit key, providing additional security against related-key attacks.

### 🔑 The Round Constant Rcon

$\text{Rcon}[j] = (\text{RC}[j], \ 0x00, \ 0x00, \ 0x00)$ where $\text{RC}[j]$ is computed in GF(2⁸) as:

$$\text{RC}[1] = 0x01, \quad \text{RC}[j] = 2 \cdot \text{RC}[j-1] \pmod{m(x)}$$

where $m(x) = x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x + 1$ is the AES irreducible polynomial (used as the reduction polynomial for field multiplication by 2).

**First 10 Rcon values:**

| $j$ | $\text{RC}[j]$ (hex) | $\text{Rcon}[j]$ (full 32-bit word) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 01 | 01000000 |
| 2 | 02 | 02000000 |
| 3 | 04 | 04000000 |
| 4 | 08 | 08000000 |
| 5 | 10 | 10000000 |
| 6 | 20 | 20000000 |
| 7 | 40 | 40000000 |
| 8 | 80 | 80000000 |
| 9 | 1B | 1B000000 |
| 10 | 36 | 36000000 |

---

## 2. The InvMixColumns Transformation on a 4-Byte Word

The **InvMixColumns** operation on a 4-byte column $[a_0, a_1, a_2, a_3]^T$ produces $[b_0, b_1, b_2, b_3]^T$ as follows:

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
b_0 \\ b_1 \\ b_2 \\ b_3
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
0E & 0B & 0D & 09 \\
09 & 0E & 0B & 0D \\
0D & 09 & 0E & 0B \\
0B & 0D & 09 & 0E
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
a_0 \\ a_1 \\ a_2 \\ a_3
\end{bmatrix}
$$

In expanded form:

$$b_0 = (0E \cdot a_0) \oplus (0B \cdot a_1) \oplus (0D \cdot a_2) \oplus (09 \cdot a_3)$$

$$b_1 = (09 \cdot a_0) \oplus (0E \cdot a_1) \oplus (0B \cdot a_2) \oplus (0D \cdot a_3)$$

$$b_2 = (0D \cdot a_0) \oplus (09 \cdot a_1) \oplus (0E \cdot a_2) \oplus (0B \cdot a_3)$$

$$b_3 = (0B \cdot a_0) \oplus (0D \cdot a_1) \oplus (09 \cdot a_2) \oplus (0E \cdot a_3)$$

All multiplications are in **GF(2⁸)** with the AES reduction polynomial $m(x) = x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x + 1$.

### ⚙️ Special Multiplication Shortcuts in GF(2⁸)

To save computation, we use these identities:

$$0E \cdot a = (02 \cdot a) \oplus (04 \cdot a) \oplus (08 \cdot a)$$

$$0B \cdot a = (01 \cdot a) \oplus (02 \cdot a) \oplus (08 \cdot a)$$

$$0D \cdot a = (01 \cdot a) \oplus (04 \cdot a) \oplus (08 \cdot a)$$

$$09 \cdot a = (01 \cdot a) \oplus (08 \cdot a)$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Trick:** You do NOT need to perform arbitrary byte multiplication. Just compute $02 \cdot a$, $04 \cdot a = 02 \cdot (02 \cdot a)$, $08 \cdot a = 02 \cdot (04 \cdot a)$, then XOR combinations to get the 0E, 0B, 0D, 09 multiplications.

---

## 3. The InvMixColumns Operation Applied to a 4-Byte Word — Algorithmic Form

To apply InvMixColumns to a single 32-bit word $w = [a_0, a_1, a_2, a_3]$, treat the four bytes as a column vector and apply the matrix transformation. The result is a new 4-byte word $w' = [b_0, b_1, b_2, b_3]$.

This is precisely the function $\text{InvMixColumns}()$ referenced in the modified decryption key schedule.

---

## 4. KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition |
|---|---------|----------------------|
| 1 | AES key sizes | 128 / 192 / 256 bits → $N_k = 4, 6, 8$ words |
| 2 | Rounds | $N_r = 10, 12, 14$ respectively |
| 3 | Total expanded key words | $N_b (N_r + 1) = 44, 52, 60$ |
| 4 | Standard recursion (non-multiple of $N_k$) | $w[i] = w[i - N_k] \oplus w[i-1]$ |
| 5 | Standard recursion (multiple of $N_k$) | $w[i] = w[i - N_k] \oplus \text{SubWord}(\text{RotWord}(w[i-1])) \oplus \text{Rcon}[i/N_k]$ |
| 6 | AES-256 extra step (when $i \equiv 4 \pmod N_k$) | $w[i] = w[i - N_k] \oplus \text{SubWord}(w[i-1])$ |
| 7 | AES reduction polynomial | $m(x) = x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x + 1$ |
| 8 | $\text{RC}[1]$ | $0x01$ |
| 9 | $\text{RC}[j]$ for $j > 1$ | $2 \cdot \text{RC}[j-1]$ in $\text{GF}(2^8)$ |
| 10 | InvMixColumns element (0E) | $0E \cdot a = (02 \cdot a) \oplus (04 \cdot a) \oplus (08 \cdot a)$ |
| 11 | InvMixColumns element (0B) | $0B \cdot a = (01 \cdot a) \oplus (02 \cdot a) \oplus (08 \cdot a)$ |
| 12 | InvMixColumns element (0D) | $0D \cdot a = (01 \cdot a) \oplus (04 \cdot a) \oplus (08 \cdot a)$ |
| 13 | InvMixColumns element (09) | $09 \cdot a = (01 \cdot a) \oplus (08 \cdot a)$ |
| 14 | Decryption: AES-128 | Same key schedule as encryption, applied in reverse order |
| 15 | Decryption: AES-192 | Apply $\text{InvMixColumns}$ to round keys 1 through $N_r - 1$ |
| 16 | Decryption: AES-256 | Apply $\text{InvMixColumns}$ to round keys 1, 3, 5, ..., $N_r - 1$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** For AES-256, students often apply InvMixColumns to *all* round keys or to *even-numbered* ones. The correct rule is **odd-numbered round keys (1, 3, 5, ..., N_r − 1)** — that is, those round keys that would be XORed with state *after* an InvMixColumns operation.

---

## 5. Engineering Utility of the Modified Key Schedule

- **Hardware Implementation:** In AES decryption chips (e.g., for SSDs, smart cards, secure bootloaders), the modified key schedule allows the *same* decryption engine to be designed with the *same* round-function structure as encryption, just with S-box replaced and round keys read backwards. This **drastically reduces silicon area**.
- **Cryptographic Libraries:** OpenSSL, mbedTLS, and other libraries use this modified schedule for AES-192/256 to enable a single unified decryption routine.
- **Security:** The fact that the key schedule is invertible and structured ensures the cipher remains **deterministic** — the same key always produces the same decryption.

---

## 6. Logical Flow Summary

**Encryption (Forward) Round Structure (for $1 \le r \le N_r - 1$):**
1. SubBytes
2. ShiftRows
3. MixColumns
4. AddRoundKey (using $K_r$)

**Decryption (Inverse) Round Structure (for $N_r - 1 \ge r \ge 1$):**
1. InvShiftRows
2. InvSubBytes
3. AddRoundKey (using $K_r$) — **for AES-192/256, this uses the modified key**
4. InvMixColumns

Notice: the order of AddRoundKey and InvMixColumns is **swapped** compared to the round structure in encryption, hence the need for the **modified key schedule**.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Equivalent Inverse Cipher** form (used in standard AES diagrams) reorders operations to put AddRoundKey first, then InvMixColumns. This is why the modified key schedule is required.

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

## 🎯 Example 1: Compute InvMixColumns for a Given 4-Byte Word

**Problem:** Given the word $w = [0x2A, 0x55, 0xC3, 0x14]$, compute $w' = \text{InvMixColumns}(w)$.

### Step 1: Identify the four bytes
$$a_0 = 0x2A, \quad a_1 = 0x55, \quad a_2 = 0xC3, \quad a_3 = 0x14$$

### Step 2: Compute all necessary GF(2⁸) products

Using the shortcuts:

**For $0x2A$:**
- $0x01 \cdot 0x2A = 0x2A$
- $0x02 \cdot 0x2A$: $0x2A \ll 1 = 0x54$, no overflow (high bit = 0), so result = $0x54$
- $0x04 \cdot 0x2A = 02 \cdot (02 \cdot 0x2A) = 02 \cdot 0x54 = 0xA8$
- $0x08 \cdot 0x2A = 02 \cdot (04 \cdot 0x2A) = 02 \cdot 0xA8 = 0x50 \oplus 0x1B = 0x4B$ (because $0xA8 \ll 1$ overflows: $(0xA8 \ll 1) \oplus 0x11B = 0x150 \oplus 0x11B = 0x04B$, but written as 8-bit: 0x50, XOR 0x1B → 0x4B)

**For $0x55$:**
- $0x01 \cdot 0x55 = 0x55$
- $0x02 \cdot 0x55$: $0x55 \ll 1 = 0xAA$, no overflow → $0xAA$
- $0x04 \cdot 0x55 = 02 \cdot 0xAA$: $0xAA \ll 1 = 0x154$, XOR $0x11B$ → $0x04F$ (i.e., $0x4F$)
- $0x08 \cdot 0x55 = 02 \cdot 0x4F$: $0x4F \ll 1 = 0x9E$, no overflow → $0x9E$

**For $0xC3$:**
- $0x01 \cdot 0xC3 = 0xC3$
- $0x02 \cdot 0xC3$: $0xC3 \ll 1 = 0x186$, XOR $0x11B$ → $0x06D$ (i.e., $0x6D$)
- $0x04 \cdot 0xC3 = 02 \cdot 0x6D$: $0x6D \ll 1 = 0xDA$, no overflow → $0xDA$
- $0x08 \cdot 0xC3 = 02 \cdot 0xDA$: $0xDA \ll 1 = 0x1B4$, XOR $0x11B$ → $0x0AB$ (i.e., $0xAB$)

**For $0x14$:**
- $0x01 \cdot 0x14 = 0x14$
- $0x02 \cdot 0x14$: $0x14 \ll 1 = 0x28$, no overflow → $0x28$
- $0x04 \cdot 0x14 = 02 \cdot 0x28$: $0x28 \ll 1 = 0x50$, no overflow → $0x50$
- $0x08 \cdot 0x14 = 02 \cdot 0x50$: $0x50 \ll 1 = 0xA0$, no overflow → $0xA0$

### Step 3: Compute the 0E, 0B, 0D, 09 multiples

For $0x2A$:
- $0x0E \cdot 0x2A = 0x54 \oplus 0xA8 \oplus 0x4B = 0x54 \oplus 0xA8 = 0xFC, \quad 0xFC \oplus 0x4B = 0xB7$
- $0x0B \cdot 0x2A = 0x2A \oplus 0x54 \oplus 0x4B = 0x2A \oplus 0x54 = 0x7E, \quad 0x7E \oplus 0x4B = 0x35$
- $0x0D \cdot 0x2A = 0x2A \oplus 0xA8 \oplus 0x4B = 0x2A \oplus 0xA8 = 0x82, \quad 0x82 \oplus 0x4B = 0xC9$
- $0x09 \cdot 0x2A = 0x2A \oplus 0x4B = 0x61$

For $0x55$:
- $0x0E \cdot 0x55 = 0xAA \oplus 0x4F \oplus 0x9E = 0xAA \oplus 0x4F = 0xE5, \quad 0xE5 \oplus 0x9E = 0x7B$
- $0x0B \cdot 0x55 = 0x55 \oplus 0xAA \oplus 0x9E = 0x55 \oplus 0xAA = 0xFF, \quad 0xFF \oplus 0x9E = 0x61$
- $0x0D \cdot 0x55 = 0x55 \oplus 0x4F \oplus 0x9E = 0x55 \oplus 0x4F = 0x1A, \quad 0x1A \oplus 0x9E = 0x84$
- $0x09 \cdot 0x55 = 0x55 \oplus 0x9E = 0xCB$

For $0xC3$:
- $0x0E \cdot 0xC3 = 0x6D \oplus 0xDA \oplus 0xAB = 0x6D \oplus 0xDA = 0xB7, \quad 0xB7 \oplus 0xAB = 0x1C$
- $0x0B \cdot 0xC3 = 0xC3 \oplus 0x6D \oplus 0xAB = 0xC3 \oplus 0x6D = 0xAE, \quad 0xAE \oplus 0xAB = 0x05$
- $0x0D \cdot 0xC3 = 0xC3 \oplus 0xDA \oplus 0xAB = 0xC3 \oplus 0xDA = 0x19, \quad 0x19 \oplus 0xAB = 0xB2$
- $0x09 \cdot 0xC3 = 0xC3 \oplus 0xAB = 0x68$

For $0x14$:
- $0x0E \cdot 0x14 = 0x28 \oplus 0x50 \oplus 0xA0 = 0x28 \oplus 0x50 = 0x78, \quad 0x78 \oplus 0xA0 = 0xD8$
- $0x0B \cdot 0x14 = 0x14 \oplus 0x28 \oplus 0xA0 = 0x14 \oplus 0x28 = 0x3C, \quad 0x3C \oplus 0xA0 = 0x9C$
- $0x0D \cdot 0x14 = 0x14 \oplus 0x50 \oplus 0xA0 = 0x14 \oplus 0x50 = 0x44, \quad 0x44 \oplus 0xA0 = 0xE4$
- $0x09 \cdot 0x14 = 0x14 \oplus 0xA0 = 0xB4$

### Step 4: Apply the InvMixColumns matrix

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_0 &= (0E \cdot a_0) \oplus (0B \cdot a_1) \oplus (0D \cdot a_2) \oplus (09 \cdot a_3) \\
&= 0xB7 \oplus 0x61 \oplus 0xB2 \oplus 0xB4 \\
&= (0xB7 \oplus 0x61) = 0xD6 \\
&\quad 0xD6 \oplus 0xB2 = 0x64 \\
&\quad 0x64 \oplus 0xB4 = 0xD0
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_1 &= (09 \cdot a_0) \oplus (0E \cdot a_1) \oplus (0B \cdot a_2) \oplus (0D \cdot a_3) \\
&= 0x61 \oplus 0x7B \oplus 0x05 \oplus 0xE4 \\
&= (0x61 \oplus 0x7B) = 0x1A \\
&\quad 0x1A \oplus 0x05 = 0x1F \\
&\quad 0x1F \oplus 0xE4 = 0xFB
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_2 &= (0D \cdot a_0) \oplus (09 \cdot a_1) \oplus (0E \cdot a_2) \oplus (0B \cdot a_3) \\
&= 0xC9 \oplus 0xCB \oplus 0x1C \oplus 0x9C \\
&= (0xC9 \oplus 0xCB) = 0x02 \\
&\quad 0x02 \oplus 0x1C = 0x1E \\
&\quad 0x1E \oplus 0x9C = 0x82
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_3 &= (0B \cdot a_0) \oplus (0D \cdot a_1) \oplus (09 \cdot a_2) \oplus (0E \cdot a_3) \\
&= 0x35 \oplus 0x84 \oplus 0x68 \oplus 0xD8 \\
&= (0x35 \oplus 0x84) = 0xB1 \\
&\quad 0xB1 \oplus 0x68 = 0xD9 \\
&\quad 0xD9 \oplus 0xD8 = 0x01
\end{aligned}
$$

### ✅ Final Result
$$w' = \text{InvMixColumns}([0x2A, 0x55, 0xC3, 0x14]) = [0xD0, 0xFB, 0x82, 0x01]$$

---

## 🎯 Example 2: AES-128 Encryption Key Expansion (Partial — First 4 Rounds)

**Given:** Cipher key in hex = `2B7E151628AED2A6ABF7158809CF4F3C`

**Step 1:** Break into 4 initial words:
- $w[0] = 0x2B7E1516$
- $w[1] = 0x28AED2A6$
- $w[2] = 0xABF71588$
- $w[3] = 0x09CF4F3C$

**Step 2:** Compute $w[4]$ (i=4, multiple of $N_k=4$):
- $\text{RotWord}(w[3]) = \text{RotWord}(0x09CF4F3C) = 0xCF4F3C09$
- $\text{SubWord}(0xCF4F3C09)$: Apply AES S-box to each byte
  - $S(0xCF) = 0x8A$
  - $S(0x4F) = 0x84$
  - $S(0x3C) = 0xEB$
  - $S(0x09) = 0x01$
  - $\text{SubWord}(...) = 0x8A84EB01$
- $\text{Rcon}[1] = 0x01000000$
- $w[4] = w[0] \oplus \text{SubWord}(\text{RotWord}(w[3])) \oplus \text{Rcon}[1]$
  - $w[4] = 0x2B7E1516 \oplus 0x8A84EB01 \oplus 0x01000000$
  - $w[4] = 0xA0FAFE17$

**Step 3:** Compute $w[5]$ (i=5, not multiple of 4):
- $w[5] = w[1] \oplus w[4] = 0x28AED2A6 \oplus 0xA0FAFE17 = 0x88542CB1$

**Step 4:** Compute $w[6]$ and $w[7]$ similarly:
- $w[6] = w[2] \oplus w[5] = 0xABF71588 \oplus 0x88542CB1 = 0x23A33939$
- $w[7] = w[3] \oplus w[6] = 0x09CF4F3C \oplus 0x23A33939 = 0x2A6C7605$

**Resulting Round Key $K_1$ (round 1):**
- $K_1 = [w[4], w[5], w[6], w[7]] = [0xA0FAFE17, 0x88542CB1, 0x23A33939, 0x2A6C7605]$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **standard AES-128 encryption key schedule**. For decryption (Inverse Cipher), these same words $K_1, K_2, \ldots, K_{10}$ are used, but applied in **reverse order** ($K_{10}$ first, $K_0$ last). No InvMixColumns modification is needed for AES-128.

---

## 🎯 Example 3: AES-256 Decryption — Apply InvMixColumns to a Round Key

**Problem:** Suppose $K_3$ for an AES-256 encryption key schedule contains the word:
$$K_3[2] = [0x52, 0x7E, 0xA1, 0xF3]$$

Since AES-256 decryption requires InvMixColumns on **odd-numbered round keys** (and $K_3$ is one of them, as $3$ is odd), we must compute:

$$K_3^{dec}[2] = \text{InvMixColumns}([0x52, 0x7E, 0xA1, 0xF3])$$

Using the same procedure as Example 1 with $a_0=0x52, a_1=0x7E, a_2=0xA1, a_3=0xF3$:

After computing the 16 individual products and XORing (left as an exercise for the student, but following the exact same procedure as Example 1), the resulting modified word becomes the **decryption round key** for that position.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why only odd-numbered round keys for AES-256?** In the Equivalent Inverse Cipher, AddRoundKey happens *before* InvMixColumns. The round keys 1, 3, 5, ..., 13 are XORed with state *after* an InvMixColumns operation in the standard inverse round structure. Pre-multiplying them by the InvMixColumns matrix **cancels out** the operation, restoring algebraic identity.

---

## 🎯 Example 4: Generate $\text{Rcon}[j]$ for $j = 1$ to $5$

$$\text{RC}[1] = 0x01$$

$$\text{RC}[2] = 2 \cdot \text{RC}[1] = 2 \cdot 0x01 = 0x02 \quad (\text{no overflow})$$

$$\text{RC}[3] = 2 \cdot \text{RC}[2] = 2 \cdot 0x02 = 0x04$$

$$\text{RC}[4] = 2 \cdot \text{RC}[3] = 2 \cdot 0x04 = 0x08$$

$$\text{RC}[5] = 2 \cdot \text{RC}[4] = 2 \cdot 0x08 = 0x10$$

Therefore:
$$\text{Rcon}[1..5] = [0x01000000, 0x02000000, 0x04000000, 0x08000000, 0x10000000]$$

---

## 🎯 Example 5: Complete Decryption Key Schedule Strategy for AES-256

**Given:** AES-256 key, $N_k = 8$, $N_r = 14$, $N_b = 4$.

**Total round keys needed:** $N_r + 1 = 15$ round keys, $K_0, K_1, \ldots, K_{14}$.

**Procedure (KTU Standard):**
1. **Step 1:** Run the standard AES-256 key expansion algorithm to obtain $w[0], w[1], \ldots, w[59]$, and group them into $K_0, K_1, \ldots, K_{14}$.
2. **Step 2:** Take the encryption key schedule and apply the transformation $K_i^{dec} = K_i$ for $i = 0$ and $i = N_r$ (i.e., first and last round keys remain unchanged).
3. **Step 3:** For $i = 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13$ (odd $i$ from 1 to 13), apply $\text{InvMixColumns}$ to **each of the four 32-bit words** of $K_i$:

$$K_i^{dec}[j] = \text{InvMixColumns}(K_i[j]) \quad \text{for } j = 0, 1, 2, 3$$

4. **Step 4:** The resulting sequence $K_0^{dec}, K_1^{dec}, \ldots, K_{14}^{dec}$ is the **decryption key schedule** for AES-256.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Always state the rule clearly — for AES-256, InvMixColumns is applied to **round keys 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13** (the odd indices between 1 and $N_r - 1 = 13$). The first and last round keys are not modified.

---
