> **Formal KTU Definition (Diffie & Hellman, 1976):**
> A *digital signature* is an authentication mechanism that enables the creator of a message to attach a code that acts as a signature. The signature is generated using the sender's **private key**, and any party can verify it using the sender's publicly available **public key**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Sealed Wax Letter

Imagine Alice wants to send a sealed letter to Bob across a dangerous kingdom:
- Alice writes the letter and puts it in a special box.
- She locks the box with a **padlock only she owns a key to** (her private key).
- Anyone walking past (Eve) can see the box but cannot open it.
- However, Bob has a **public key** that lets him verify the lock belongs to Alice (because the lock is uniquely registered to her).

If Eve tampers with the letter, the lock will be visibly broken. If Alice later denies sending the letter, Bob can prove to a judge (third party) that only Alice's unique padlock could have sealed it. **This is precisely how digital signatures work in cyberspace.**

### 1.3 Digital Signature vs. Message Authentication Code (MAC)

| Feature | Digital Signature | MAC (Message Authentication Code) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Keys Used** | Asymmetric (Public + Private) | Symmetric (Shared Secret) |
| **Provides Non-Repudiation** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| **Verifiable by Third Parties** | ✅ Anyone with public key | ❌ Only the two parties |
| **Computational Cost** | Higher (modular exponentiation) | Lower (hash + secret key) |
| **Algorithm Examples** | RSA, DSA, ECDSA | HMAC, CMAC |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** Digital signatures are essentially an *asymmetric* counterpart to MACs. The crucial academic distinction is that MACs use symmetric keys and therefore cannot provide non-repudiation — a core reason digital signatures are legally binding under acts like the IT Act, 2000 (India).

### 1.4 Digital Signature Standards (DSS)

The **Digital Signature Standard (DSS)** is a Federal Information Processing Standard (FIPS) published by the **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)** in 1994 (FIPS PUB 186). It specifies a suite of algorithms that can be used to generate digital signatures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Note:** The original DSS was based on the **Digital Signature Algorithm (DSA)**. Over time, NIST expanded DSS to include **RSA**, **Elliptic Curve DSA (ECDSA)**, and **Edwards-curve DSA (EdDSA)**. For KTU examinations, focus heavily on DSA, RSA-based signatures, and ElGamal.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Essential Properties of a Digital Signature

For a digital signature scheme to be considered cryptographically robust, it must satisfy the following properties:

- **Unforgeable:** It must be computationally infeasible to forge a signature (either by creating a new message for an existing signature or a new signature for an existing message).
- **Authentic:** The signature must convincingly prove the signer's identity.
- **Non-reusable / Non-transferable:** A signature on one document cannot be lifted and reused on another.
- **Unalterable:** Once a document is signed, it cannot be modified.
- **Non-repudiable:** The signer cannot later successfully claim they did not sign.

### 2.2 Requirements for a Digital Signature Scheme

A digital signature scheme comprises three polynomial-time algorithms:
1. **Key Generation (Gen):** Outputs a public key `pk` and secret key `sk`.
2. **Signing Algorithm (Sign):** Takes message `M` and secret key `sk` → outputs signature `σ`.
3. **Verification Algorithm (Ver):** Takes `M`, `σ`, and `pk` → outputs `accept` or `reject`.

### 2.3 Direct vs. Arbitrated Digital Signatures

#### A. Direct Digital Signatures
The communication involves **only the two communicating parties** (Alice and Bob). The signature is generated using the sender's private key and verified using the sender's public key.

- **Advantage:** Faster, no third-party delay.
- **Weakness:** Validity of the signature depends solely on the security of the sender's private key. If a key is compromised, an adversary can forge signatures. Also, the sender can **repudiate** by claiming the key was stolen.

#### B. Arbitrated Digital Signatures
Every signed message from sender Alice first goes to a trusted **arbitrator/CA (Certification Authority)**. The arbitrator checks the message, timestamp, and signature, then forwards it to Bob along with a confirmation.

- **Advantage:** Resolves repudiation disputes; provides stronger non-repudiation.
- **Weakness:** Adds latency; the arbitrator becomes a single point of failure and a bottleneck.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Concept:** Direct signatures are the *practical standard* (used in PGP, email signing). Arbitrated signatures are *conceptually important* but rarely deployed due to overhead. Always mention both in essay-type questions.

### 2.4 Attacks and Forgeries on Digital Signatures

A **forgery** is the successful creation of a fraudulent signature. The hierarchy of attacks (from hardest to defend against):

1. **Key-Only Attack:** Adversary only knows the public key.
2. **Known Message Attack:** Adversary has signatures on a set of known messages.
3. **Chosen Message Attack:** Adversary can obtain signatures on messages of their choice (before attempting forgery).
4. **Adaptive Chosen Message Attack:** Adversary can query signatures adaptively.

**Types of Forgeries:**
- **Total Break:** Adversary recovers the private key.
- **Universal Forgery:** Adversary can forge signatures for *any* message.
- **Selective Forgery:** Adversary can forge signatures for a chosen subset of messages.
- **Existential Forgery:** Adversary can forge at least *one* valid signature for *some* message (does not get to choose the message).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL CONSTRAINT:** In the formula table below, vertical bars for absolute value are written using `\vert` to prevent markdown table corruption. Use these exact symbols in your exam scripts.

| Algorithm | Operation | Mathematical Equation | Domain / Constraints |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **DSA Key Gen** | Public Key | $y = g^{x} \bmod p$ | $x \in \{1,\dots,q-1\}$ |
| **DSA Sign** | Component r | $r = (g^{k} \bmod p) \bmod q$ | $k \in \{1,\dots,q-1\}$ random |
| **DSA Sign** | Component s | $s = k^{-1}\,(H(M) + x \cdot r) \bmod q$ | $H(M)$ is hash digest |
| **DSA Verify** | Inverse Weight | $w = s^{-1} \bmod q$ | — |
| **DSA Verify** | Intermediate u1 | $u_{1} = H(M) \cdot w \bmod q$ | — |
| **DSA Verify** | Intermediate u2 | $u_{2} = r \cdot w \bmod q$ | — |
| **DSA Verify** | Check Value | $v = (g^{u_{1}} \cdot y^{u_{2}} \bmod p) \bmod q$ | Accept if $v = r$ |
| **RSA Sign** | Signing | $S = M^{d} \bmod n$ | $n = p \cdot q$ |
| **RSA Verify** | Verification | $M = S^{e} \bmod n$ | Accept if recovered M matches |
| **ElGamal Sign** | r-component | $r = g^{k} \bmod p$ | $\gcd(k, p-1) = 1$ |
| **ElGamal Sign** | s-component | $s = (H(M) - x \cdot r) \cdot k^{-1} \bmod (p-1)$ | — |
| **ElGamal Verify** | Check | $g^{H(M)} \equiv y^{r} \cdot r^{s} \pmod p$ | — |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Digital signatures underpin:
- **Software Code Signing:** Authenticates binaries (e.g., Microsoft Authenticode, Linux package signing).
- **TLS/SSL Handshakes:** ECDSA is used in modern HTTPS connections.
- **Blockchain & Cryptocurrency:** Bitcoin and Ethereum use ECDSA over the secp256k1 curve.
- **Government E-Governance:** India's eSign framework, Aadhaar-based eKYC, GST filing.
- **Document Workflows:** PDF digital signatures, legally binding e-contracts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 RSA Digital Signature Scheme — Complete Derivation

The RSA signature scheme is the simplest asymmetric signature. The key generation mirrors RSA encryption, but the roles of `e` and `d` are inverted.

#### 3.1.1 Key Generation

Let Alice choose:
- Two large distinct primes $p$ and $q$.
- Compute modulus $n = p \cdot q$.
- Compute Euler's totient $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$.
- Choose public exponent $e$ such that $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$.
- Compute private exponent $d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)}$.

The **public key is $(e, n)$** and the **private key is $d$**.

#### 3.1.2 Signing Algorithm (Alice)

Alice takes the message $M$ (where $0 \leq M < n$) and computes:

$$
S = M^{d} \bmod n
$$

Alice transmits $(M, S)$ to Bob.

#### 3.1.3 Verification Algorithm (Bob)

Bob uses Alice's public key $(e, n)$ to recover the original message:

$$
M' = S^{e} \bmod n
$$

If $M' = M$, the signature is **valid**; otherwise, it is **rejected**.

#### 3.1.4 Mathematical Correctness Proof

The correctness rests on the RSA property. We start with the signature equation:

$$
S^{e} \bmod n
$$

Substituting $S = M^{d} \bmod n$:

$$
S^{e} \bmod n \equiv (M^{d})^{e} \bmod n
$$

Applying exponent associativity:

$$
\equiv M^{d \cdot e} \bmod n
$$

By construction of $d$, we know that $d \cdot e \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$. Therefore:

$$
\equiv M^{1 + k \cdot \phi(n)} \bmod n
$$

Using Euler's theorem, $M^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod n$ (when $\gcd(M, n) = 1$):

$$
\equiv M^{1} \cdot (M^{\phi(n)})^{k} \bmod n
$$

$$
\equiv M \cdot 1^{k} \bmod n \equiv M \bmod n
$$

Therefore, the verification always recovers the original message. ∎

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In practice, RSA signatures are applied to **hashes of messages**, not raw messages. A real signing equation is $S = H(M)^{d} \bmod n$. Signing raw messages is insecure because an adversary can forge signatures by choosing $S$ first and computing $M = S^{e} \bmod n$.

---

### 3.2 Digital Signature Algorithm (DSA) — Complete Derivation

DSA is the heart of the FIPS DSS standard. It is a variant of the ElGamal and Schnorr signature schemes, but with a smaller output.

#### 3.2.1 Global Public Parameters

All users in the system share:
- A prime modulus $p$ of size $L$ bits (typically $L = 1024$, now $L = 2048$ or $3072$).
- A prime divisor $q$ of $p - 1$, where $2^{159} < q < 2^{160}$ (160-bit).
- A generator $g$ of the multiplicative subgroup of order $q$ in $\mathbb{Z}_{p}^{*}$, computed as:

$$
g = h^{(p-1)/q} \bmod p
$$

where $h$ is any integer with $1 < h < p - 1$ such that $h^{(p-1)/q} \bmod p > 1$.

- A cryptographic hash function $H(\cdot)$ — SHA-2 or SHA-3 in modern implementations.

#### 3.2.2 Per-User Key Generation

Each user (Alice) generates:
- A random private key $x \in \{1, 2, \dots, q - 1\}$.
- Computes her public key $y$ as:

$$
y = g^{x} \bmod p
$$

#### 3.2.3 Signature Generation (Alice)

**Step 1:** Pick a fresh, secret random integer $k$ with $1 \leq k \leq q - 1$. This $k$ must be unique per signature.

**Step 2:** Compute the first signature component:

$$
r = (g^{k} \bmod p) \bmod q
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the double modulo?** The first $\bmod p$ reduces the value to fit in $\mathbb{Z}_{p}$, and the second $\bmod q$ projects the result into the smaller subgroup of order $q$.

**Step 3:** Compute the modular inverse of $k$ modulo $q$:

$$
k^{-1} \bmod q
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the second signature component using the hash of the message:

$$
s = k^{-1} \cdot \big(H(M) + x \cdot r\big) \bmod q
$$

The signature on message $M$ is the pair $(r, s)$.

#### 3.2.4 Signature Verification (Bob)

Bob receives $(M, r, s)$. He performs:

**Step 1:** Verify the domain constraints:

$$
0 < r < q \quad \text{and} \quad 0 < s < q
$$

If either condition fails, the signature is **invalid**.

**Step 2:** Compute the modular inverse of $s$:

$$
w = s^{-1} \bmod q
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the two verification intermediates:

$$
u_{1} = H(M) \cdot w \bmod q
$$

$$
u_{2} = r \cdot w \bmod q
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the verification value:

$$
v = (g^{u_{1}} \cdot y^{u_{2}} \bmod p) \bmod q
$$

**Step 5:** Accept the signature **if and only if $v = r$**.

#### 3.2.5 Mathematical Correctness Proof

We need to show that when Alice signed honestly, Bob's check will succeed. We begin from Alice's definition of $s$:

$$
s \cdot k \equiv H(M) + x \cdot r \pmod{q}
$$

Multiply both sides by $k^{-1}$:

$$
k \equiv s^{-1}\,(H(M) + x \cdot r) \pmod{q}
$$

Now substitute into Bob's intermediate $u_1$ and $u_2$:

$$
u_{1} + u_{2} \cdot x \equiv H(M) \cdot w + r \cdot w \cdot x \pmod{q}
$$

$$
\equiv w \cdot (H(M) + r \cdot x) \pmod{q}
$$

Substituting the definition of $w = s^{-1}$:

$$
\equiv s^{-1} \cdot (H(M) + r \cdot x) \pmod{q}
$$

But from Alice's signing equation, $s \equiv k^{-1}(H(M) + x \cdot r) \pmod{q}$, so:

$$
s^{-1} \cdot (H(M) + r \cdot x) \equiv k \pmod{q}
$$

Therefore:

$$
u_{1} + u_{2} \cdot x \equiv k \pmod{q}
$$

Now compute Bob's $v$:

$$
v = (g^{u_{1}} \cdot y^{u_{2}} \bmod p) \bmod q
$$

Substitute $y = g^{x}$:

$$
g^{u_{1}} \cdot y^{u_{2}} = g^{u_{1}} \cdot g^{x \cdot u_{2}} = g^{u_{1} + x \cdot u_{2}} \pmod{p}
$$

Using the congruence derived above, $u_{1} + x \cdot u_{2} \equiv k \pmod{q}$. Since $g$ has order $q$:

$$
g^{u_{1} + x \cdot u_{2}} \equiv g^{k} \pmod{p}
$$

Hence:

$$
v = (g^{k} \bmod p) \bmod q = r
$$

The verification succeeds. ∎

---

### 3.3 ElGamal Digital Signature Scheme — Complete Derivation

ElGamal (1985) predates DSA and forms the theoretical basis for it. It outputs a signature that is *twice as long* as the message hash.

#### 3.3.1 Key Generation

- Public parameters: prime $p$, primitive root $g$ modulo $p$.
- Alice's private key: $x \in \{1, \dots, p-2\}$.
- Alice's public key: $y = g^{x} \bmod p$.

#### 3.3.2 Signature Generation

Alice chooses random $k$ coprime to $p-1$. She computes:

$$
r = g^{k} \bmod p
$$

$$
s = (H(M) - x \cdot r) \cdot k^{-1} \bmod (p-1)
$$

Signature is $(r, s)$.

#### 3.3.3 Verification

Bob checks $0 < r < p$ and $0 < s < p-1$, then computes:

$$
v_{1} = g^{H(M)} \bmod p
$$

$$
v_{2} = y^{r} \cdot r^{s} \bmod p
$$

Accept if $v_{1} = v_{2}$.

#### 3.3.4 Proof of Correctness

Starting from $s$:

$$
s \cdot k \equiv H(M) - x \cdot r \pmod{p-1}
$$

Adding $x \cdot r$ to both sides:

$$
s \cdot k + x \cdot r \equiv H(M) \pmod{p-1}
$$

Therefore, modulo $p$:

$$
g^{s \cdot k + x \cdot r} \equiv g^{H(M)} \pmod{p}
$$

Splitting the exponent:

$$
(g^{k})^{s} \cdot (g^{x})^{r} \equiv g^{H(M)} \pmod{p}
$$

Substituting $r = g^{k} \bmod p$ and $y = g^{x} \bmod p$:

$$
r^{s} \cdot y^{r} \equiv g^{H(M)} \pmod{p}
$$

Thus $v_{2} = v_{1}$, confirming the signature. ∎

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Direct Digital Signature Flow

The diagram below illustrates the direct digital signature process where Alice signs the message using her private key and Bob verifies using her public key.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Original Message M"] --> B["Hash Function H of M"]
    B --> C["Digest H of M"]
    C --> D["Sign with Private Key of Alice"]
    A --> E["Append Signature S"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["Signed Document Transmitted"]
    F --> G["Bob Receives Signed Document"]
    G --> H["Separate Signature and Message"]
    H --> I["Hash Received Message"]
    I --> J["Hash of Received M"]
    H --> K["Decrypt Signature using Public Key of Alice"]
    K --> L["Recovered Digest"]
    J --> M["Compare Digests"]
    L --> M
    M --> N["Decision Accept or Reject"]
```

### 4.2 Arbitrated Digital Signature Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Alice Prepares Message M"] --> B["Alice Signs Message using Private Key"]
    B --> C["Signed Message Sent to Arbitrator"]
    C --> D["Arbitrator Verifies Signature and Content"]
    D --> E{"Signature Valid?"}
    E -- "No" --> F["Reject and Notify Alice"]
    E -- "Yes" --> G["Arbitrator Attaches Timestamp and Own Signature"]
    G --> H["Arbitrator Sends to Bob"]
    H --> I["Bob Verifies Arbitrator Signature"]
    I --> J["Bob Verifies Alice Signature using Public Key"]
    J --> K["Message Accepted by Bob"]
```

### 4.3 DSA Functional Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph KeyGen["Key Generation Phase"]
        K1["Choose random x in 1 to q minus 1"] --> K2["Compute y equals g power x mod p"]
        K2 --> K3["Public Key y and Private Key x"]
    end

    subgraph SignGen["Signing Phase"]
        S1["Choose random k in 1 to q minus 1"] --> S2["Compute r equals g power k mod p mod q"]
        S2 --> S3["Compute s equals k inverse times H of M plus x times r mod q"]
        S3 --> S4["Signature pair r and s"]
    end

    subgraph Verif["Verification Phase"]
        V1["Receive Message M and Signature r and s"] --> V2["Compute w equals s inverse mod q"]
        V2 --> V3["Compute u1 equals H of M times w mod q"]
        V3 --> V4["Compute u2 equals r times w mod q"]
        V4 --> V5["Compute v equals g power u1 times y power u2 mod p mod q"]
        V5 --> V6{"v equals r?"}
        V6 -- "Yes" --> V7["Accept Signature as Valid"]
        V6 -- "No" --> V8["Reject Signature as Invalid"]
    end

    K3 --> SignGen
    S4 --> Verif
```

### 4.4 Attack Hierarchy on Digital Signatures

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Adversary Capabilities"] --> B["Key Only Attack"]
    A --> C["Known Message Attack"]
    A --> D["Chosen Message Attack"]
    A --> E["Adaptive Chosen Message Attack"]
    B --> F["Hardest for Adversary"]
    E --> G["Easiest for Adversary"]
    F --> H["Strongest Security for Signer"]
    G --> I["Weakest Security for Signer"]
```

### 4.5 Comparative Block Diagram of Signature Schemes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RSA["RSA Signature Scheme"] --> RSA1["Output Length equals Modulus n"]
    RSA1 --> RSA2["Security based on Integer Factorization"]
    DSA["DSA Digital Signature Algorithm"] --> DSA1["Output Length equals 2 times q"]
    DSA1 --> DSA2["Security based on Discrete Logarithm Problem"]
    ELG["ElGamal Signature Scheme"] --> ELG1["Output Length equals 2 times p"]
    ELG1 --> ELG2["Security based on Discrete Logarithm Problem"]
    ECDSA["ECDSA Elliptic Curve DSA"] --> ECDSA1["Output Length equals 2 times n"]
    ECDSA1 --> ECDSA2["Security based on Elliptic Curve DLP"]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
*List any three properties that a digital signature scheme must satisfy.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Unforgeability:** It must be computationally infeasible for any adversary to forge a signature without knowing the signer's private key. (1 mark)
2. **Authenticity:** The signature must uniquely identify the signer and cannot be replicated. (1 mark)
3. **Non-repudiation:** The signer cannot later deny having signed the document; the signature is legally binding. (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Differentiate between a Message Authentication Code (MAC) and a Digital Signature.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | MAC | Digital Signature |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Key Type | Symmetric (shared secret) | Asymmetric (public + private) |
| Non-Repudiation | Not supported | Fully supported |
| Verification | Only by parties sharing secret | By anyone holding the public key |

(1 mark per valid comparison point. For full 3 marks, present at least three clear distinctions.)

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

**(a) Describe the Digital Signature Standard (DSS) and explain how the Digital Signature Algorithm (DSA) works. List the steps for key generation, signing, and verification. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Digital Signature Standard (DSS)** was published by NIST in 1994 (FIPS PUB 186) as a U.S. federal standard for digital signatures. It originally specified the Digital Signature Algorithm (DSA), and later revisions added RSA, ECDSA, and EdDSA.

**DSA Key Generation (1 mark):**
- Public parameters: prime $p$ (1024+ bits), prime divisor $q$ of $p-1$ (160 bits), generator $g = h^{(p-1)/q} \bmod p$.
- Each user selects private key $x \in \{1, \dots, q-1\}$.
- Computes public key $y = g^{x} \bmod p$.

**DSA Signing (3 marks):**
- Select random per-message secret $k$ with $1 \leq k \leq q-1$.
- Compute $r = (g^{k} \bmod p) \bmod q$.
- Compute $s = k^{-1} (H(M) + x \cdot r) \bmod q$.
- Signature is the pair $(r, s)$.

**DSA Verification (3 marks):**
- Compute $w = s^{-1} \bmod q$.
- Compute $u_1 = H(M) \cdot w \bmod q$, $u_2 = r \cdot w \bmod q$.
- Compute $v = (g^{u_1} \cdot y^{u_2} \bmod p) \bmod q$.
- Accept signature if and only if $v = r$.

---

**(b) Explain the RSA digital signature scheme with a neat mathematical proof of correctness. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**RSA Key Generation (1 mark):**
- Choose two primes $p$, $q$. Compute $n = p \cdot q$.
- Compute $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$.
- Choose $e$ with $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$. Compute $d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)}$.
- Public key: $(e, n)$; Private key: $d$.

**RSA Signing (1.5 marks):**
Alice signs message $M$ (where $0 \leq M < n$) using her private key:
$$S = M^{d} \bmod n$$

**RSA Verification (1.5 marks):**
Bob recovers the message using Alice's public key:
$$M' = S^{e} \bmod n$$
Accept if $M' = M$.

**Mathematical Proof of Correctness (3 marks):**
Starting from the signature $S = M^{d} \bmod n$, Bob computes:
$$S^{e} \bmod n \equiv (M^{d})^{e} \bmod n \equiv M^{d \cdot e} \bmod n$$

Since $d \cdot e \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, we have $d \cdot e = 1 + k\phi(n)$ for some integer $k$. Substituting:
$$M^{d \cdot e} \bmod n = M^{1 + k\phi(n)} \bmod n = M \cdot (M^{\phi(n)})^{k} \bmod n$$

By Euler's theorem, $M^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$ (provided $\gcd(M, n) = 1$). Thus:
$$M \cdot 1^{k} \bmod n = M \bmod n$$

The recovered $M'$ equals the original $M$, confirming signature validity. ∎

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common mistake students make is *not* applying the hash before signing in RSA. Always write $S = H(M)^{d} \bmod n$ in your answer. Signing raw messages allows existential forgery attacks. Additionally, students often forget to verify the recovered message matches the *original hash*, not the plaintext. (Lose 1–2 marks for this.)

---

#### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a) Explain the ElGamal digital signature scheme with its key generation, signing, and verification algorithms. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Global Parameters (1 mark):**
- Prime $p$, primitive root $g$ of $\mathbb{Z}_{p}^{*}$.

**Key Generation (1.5 marks):**
- Choose private key $x \in \{1, \dots, p-2\}$.
- Compute public key $y = g^{x} \bmod p$.

**Signing Algorithm (2.5 marks):**
- Choose random $k$ with $\gcd(k, p-1) = 1$.
- Compute $r = g^{k} \bmod p$.
- Compute $s = (H(M) - x \cdot r) \cdot k^{-1} \bmod (p-1)$.
- Signature is the pair $(r, s)$.

**Verification Algorithm (2 marks):**
- Check $0 < r < p$ and $0 < s < p-1$.
- Compute $v_1 = g^{H(M)} \bmod p$.
- Compute $v_2 = y^{r} \cdot r^{s} \bmod p$.
- Accept if $v_1 = v_2$.

---

**(b) Discuss the various types of attacks and forgeries possible against digital signature schemes. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Types of Attacks (4 marks):**
1. **Key-Only Attack:** Adversary knows only the public verification key. The hardest attack for the adversary; schemes secure against this are the strongest. (1 mark)
2. **Known Message Attack:** Adversary possesses signatures for a set of messages chosen by the signer. Used to model leakage of signatures from public channels. (1 mark)
3. **Chosen Message Attack:** Adversary can choose specific messages and obtain signatures for them before attempting forgery. Stronger than known message. (1 mark)
4. **Adaptive Chosen Message Attack:** Adversary can query signatures on messages *based on previously obtained signatures*. The strongest practical attack model. (1 mark)

**Types of Forgeries (3 marks):**
1. **Total Break:** Adversary recovers the signer's private key. (1 mark)
2. **Universal Forgery:** Adversary can forge signatures for *any* arbitrary message. (0.5 mark)
3. **Selective Forgery:** Adversary can forge for messages chosen from a specific subset. (0.5 mark)
4. **Existential Forgery:** Adversary can produce a valid (message, signature) pair, but does not control which message. (0.5 mark)
5. **Schemes secure against existential forgery under adaptive chosen message attack (EUF-CMA) are considered practically robust.** (0.5 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently confuse the *attack types* (adversary's knowledge) with *forgery types* (outcome achieved). Make a clear distinction in your answer — **attacks are about input, forgeries are about output**. Also avoid using the term "non-repudiation attack" — this is informal. Use standard cryptographic terms. (Lose 1–2 marks for vague terminology.)

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Fire Revision Points — Memorize Before Every KTU Exam:**

- [ ] A **digital signature** provides **authentication + integrity + non-repudiation** (the "trinity" — always mention all three).
- [ ] **Direct signatures** = no third party; **arbitrated signatures** = trusted CA involved.
- [ ] **MAC vs Digital Signature:** MAC uses symmetric keys (no non-repudiation); digital signature uses asymmetric keys.
- [ ] **RSA Signature:** Sign with $d$, Verify with $e$. Always hash before signing.
- [ ] **DSA Signature:** Components are $r = (g^{k} \bmod p) \bmod q$ and $s = k^{-1}(H(M) + xr) \bmod q$.
- [ ] **DSA Verification:** $w = s^{-1}$, then $v = (g^{u_1} y^{u_2} \bmod p) \bmod q$, accept if $v = r$.
- [ ] **ElGamal Signature:** $r = g^{k} \bmod p$, $s = (H(M) - xr) \cdot k^{-1} \bmod (p-1)$. Verify via $g^{H(M)} \equiv y^{r} r^{s} \pmod{p}$.
- [ ] **Randomness is critical:** In both DSA and ElGamal, the per-message random $k$ must be **secret, fresh, and uniformly random**. Reusing $k$ leaks the private key.
- [ ] **DSS** is a U.S. NIST standard (FIPS 186). Original version uses DSA; later versions add RSA, ECDSA, EdDSA.
- [ ] **Security foundations:** RSA → Integer Factorization Problem; DSA/ElGamal → Discrete Logarithm Problem; ECDSA → Elliptic Curve DLP.
- [ ] **Forgery hierarchy (worst to mildest):** Total Break → Universal → Selective → Existential.
- [ ] **Signature sizes:** DSA produces 320-bit signatures (two 160-bit values); RSA produces modulus-sized signatures; ElGamal produces twice-modulus sized signatures (hence DSA's improvement).
- [ ] Always remember to apply a **cryptographic hash** to the message before signing — never sign raw plaintext in practice.
